1: Evaluating the environmental and economic sustainability of energy from anaerobic digestion of different feedstocks in Turkey
Gulizar Balcioglu, Harish K. Jeswani, Adisa Azapagic Sustainable Industrials Systems, Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Manchester, Manchester M13 9PL, UK

A R T I C INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 6 February 2022 Received in revised form 5 June 2022 Accepted 6 June 2022 Available online 9 June 2022 Editor: Prof. Shabbir Gheewala Keywords: Biogas Organic waste Manure Maize silage Life cycle assessment Life cycle costing|Anaerobic digestion (AD) of organic wastes is recognised as a promising solution to reduce GHG emissions owing to its multiple functions, such as energy supply, waste treatment and production of organic fertiliser. Being the 7th largest country for agricultural production, Turkey produces large amounts of agricultural wastes and live- stock manure, most of which is underutilised or managed in unsustainable ways causing adverse effects on the environment. Thus, this study aims to evaluate the environmental and economic sustainability of heat and power cogenerated through AD of different types of waste in Turkey. For this purpose, life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle costing (LCC) have been carried out for biogas plants utilising animal manure (cattle and chicken), organic wastes (vegetable and slaughterhouse wastes) and crop maize silage. Using feedstocks with high solid content and biogas yield (i.e., organic wastes and chicken manure) leads to lower environmental im- pacts than for the current energy systems (heat from natural gas and electricity from the grid) in 15 out of 17 cat- egories considered, including climate change. On the other hand, due to the lower organic-matter content and biogas yield, bioenergy from cattle slurry (with or without maize silage co-digestion) has 40-70% higher impacts than the current energy systems for climate change, photochemical oxidants formation and particulates, in addi- tion to a much higher acidification and marine eutrophication (
2: Global concerns related to environmental protection and energy se- curity have led to a growing interest in the deployment of renewable energy around the world. Among them, organic waste has attracted a lot of attention for being abundant and having a potential to contribute towards climate change mitigation, sustainable waste management and rural development. Therefore, it can help address almost all sustainable development goals of the United Nations by creating potential benefits for the environment (UN, 2015), as well as economic and social devel- opment (IEA, 2017).

In this context, biogas production via anaerobic digestion (AD) of or- ganic materials is recognised as one of the well-established and effective ways to reduce GHG emissions (AEBIOM, 2017), particularly because of the associated reduction in environmental impacts from traditional waste management and mineral fertiliser production (Bacenetti et al., 2016). Organic substrates, sourced either from waste streams or pur- posefully grown crops, can be transformed effectively into biogas in an- aerobic digesters (Vasco-Correa et al., 2018). Biogas, consisting largely of methane (50-70%), can substitute fossil fuels by either burning it di- rectly in an on-site cogeneration plant to produce heat and electricity or by upgrading to biomethane to use instead of natural gas (AEBIOM, 2017; WEC, 2016). Moreover, digestate from AD plants can provide a sustainable source of nutrients in agriculture by displacing conventional mineral fertilisers (Drosg et al., 2015). Therefore, it can help address almost all sustainable development goals of the United Nations by creating potential benefits for the environment (UN, 2015), as well as economic and social devel- opment (IEA, 2017).

Moreover, digestate from AD plants can provide a sustainable source of nutrients in agriculture by displacing conventional mineral fertilisers (Drosg et al., 2015). biogas energy supply by five times (to 1.5 EJ/yr) between 2000 and 2018 (IEA, 2020; REN21, 2019). This trend is expected to continue and reach up to ten times of today's level in 2050 as <10% of current biogas potential is utilised (IEA, 2020; IRENA, 2020).

With the development of energy and environmental policies supporting the use of renewables, there has also been a growing interest in the AD of agricultural residues for bioenergy in Turkey (MENR, 2014; MofEU, 2012; WMD, 2015). Being a party to the Paris Agreement, Turkey has set ambitious targets to reduce GHG emissions (by 21 % by 2030 compared to 2012; UNFCC, 2015) and increase the installed capac- ity of renewable energy (by 65 % by 2023), including 1000 MW from biomass (EMRA, 2018a; MENR, 2014, 2019). Considering Turkey's posi- tion in the global agricultural sector (7th largest agricultural producer in the world and 2nd largest chicken producer in Europe; INVEST, 2016), a huge amount of agricultural wastes is generated from farm activities and food wastes from the retail stage (Salihoglu et al., 2018). According to the latest estimates, around 180 Mt of animal manure is annually pro- duced in Turkey (GDRE, 2022), but <10% of it is converted into energy. In 2018, the country had 52 AD plants, with the total installed capacity of 95 MW, most of which were small-scale MWe) (GDRE, 2018). Similar to global trends (Bacenetti et al., 2016), animal manure is the most prevailing source used (either solely or combined with other feedstocks) in 70 of AD plants. Although energy crops are commonly used as feedstock in AD plants in Europe because of their high biogas yield (Ingrao et al., 2019), there are no plants using energy crops in Turkey yet. Furthermore, very little is currently known about the

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been widely used to evaluate the en- vironmental sustainability of biogas plants, with various studies reporting that energy generation from AD systems has lower GHG emis- sions as compared to fossil fuels (e.g. Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016). However, the performance of AD plants varies depending on many fac- tors, including feedstock type, operational parameters, biogas utilisation pathways and digestate treatment methods (Agostini et al., 2015), as well as the energy technologies displaced (Beausang et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019). All of these are also highly influenced by regional and spatial parameters, such as biomass availability, agricultural prac- tices and the infrastructure (Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016; Salvador et al., 2018). For example, in their review of LCA studies of 15 AD plants in Europe, Hijazi et al. (2016) found the plant scale, feedstock properties and regional factors as the most important parameters for biogas environmental sustainability. Geographical aspects were also found in the review by Aziz et al. (2019), which covered 48 papers, to have strong influence on LCA results, together with system boundaries. Moreover, Bacenetti et al. (2016), Rajendran and Murthy (2019) and Salvador et al. (2018) noted that most of LCA studies were based in Europe, so further studies are necessary to increase the understanding of the sustainability of biogas in other regions.
sustainability of energy from biogas in Turkey and to what degree it contributes to the national targets for GHG emissions reduction.

Along with the regional aspects, methodological choices, such as sys- tem boundaries, functional unit and databases, have critical importance for LCA results. For instance, adding a co-substrate, such as maize silage, to animal slurry usually improves the environmental sustainability of AD due to the higher biogas yield (Esteves et al., 2019). However, reducing the amount of manure going into the AD process by adding a co-substrate could reverse the results in comparison to the mono- digestion of manure since a lower amount of manure means lower benefits from the avoided impacts of manure storage (Lovarelli et al., 2019). Further, the biogas conversion efficiency benefits arising from adding a co-substrate can be offset due to longer feedstock-transport distances (Cusenza et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019) or indirect effects in sectors, such as competition with food and feed production, depend- ing on the substrate used (Cusenza et al., 2021). In this regard, if energy crops substitute animal manure, there is also a potential net increase in impacts due to their cultivation (Beausang et al., 2021) and possible land-use change impacts which are rarely addressed in literature (Esteves et al., 2019). For example, Zhang et al. (2021) found that co- digestion of pig manure with grass silage had lower life cycle impacts than its mono-digestion, while Beausang et al. (2021) revealed that the use of grass silage should be restricted when consequential LCA impacts from the intensification of grass production through additional nitrogen fertilisation were taken into account.

Limited number of studies evaluating life cycle economic implica- tions of AD plants is available, (Hamzehkolaei and Amjady, 2018; Lauer et al., 2017; Usack et al., 2017), as the majority has focused on ei- ther cost-benefit- or techno-economic analysis (Venanzi et al., 2018). These include net present value, payback time and internal rate of re- turn, in addition to electricity generation costs. Further, recent studies (Hamedani et al., 2020; Lovarelli et al., 2019; Zhang and Xu, 2020) have accounted for the monetary values of environmental impacts, par- ticularly GHG emission reduction costs (i.e. carbon credits), attempting to provide a more holistic view on the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants. A large difference in the electricity pro- duction costs via biogas ($25-$280/MWh.) was reported across different European countries (IEA/NEA, 2015), which in some cases can be higher than costs of fossil fuels (Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2014), depending on different parameters. For example, similar to re- sults found in Saracevic et al. (2019) for different biogas plants in Austria, Venanzi et al. (2018) found that operating and capital costs were the main reason for high costs of electricity from AD plants at a dairy farm in Italy. Lovarelli et al. (2019) and Hamedani et al. (2020) identified the economic profitability of a biogas plant in Italy was

related mainly to the electricity production and associated subsidies, in- cluding carbon credits; thus, larger plants were more profitable than the smaller. Similarly, in an economic analysis of biogas plants operated with food waste in the UK (Slorach et al., 2019a) and with organic household waste in Canada (Sanscartier et al., 2012), the plant scale was found to be an important factor as larger plants were more eco- nomic and financially viable compared to small-scale installations. However, it is still difficult to draw general conclusions on the sustain- ability of biogas plants as different approaches were taken in each study based on regional conditions (e.g., climate, farm practices, eco- nomic parameters), along with some methodological differences (e.g., by-product allocation and system boundaries).

In addition to the plant scale, the feedstock also has a significant ef- fect on the economic performance of biogas plants (Saracevic et al., 2019). For example, in a US study, higher organic loading of substrate in the co-digestion of animal manure was found economically better than manure-only digestion (Usack et al., 2017). However, as men- tioned above in the discussion on LCA, the use of energy crops as a co- substrate could counterbalance the environmental benefits as studies in Italy (Lovarelli et al., 2019) and Austria (Saracevic et al., 2019) dem- onstrated.

In Turkey, only one LCA study of biogas plants has been carried out (Nayal al., 2016), whereas several studies have focused on the eco- nomic assessment, either in a specific region (Akbulut, 2012; Balaman and Selim, 2014) or across the country (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil, 2021). Among these, Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Akbulut (2012) evaluated both the GHG savings and feasi- bility of electrity generation from biogas. The LCA study by Nayal et al. (2016) assessed the environmental sustainability of a small-scale (330 kWe) biogas plant using agricultural and farm wastes considering six indicators. The authors found that power derived from biogas had lower impacts compared to those from coal power plant; the digestate application was the main hotspot for the climate change impact and eu- trophication. However, it is unknown how the biogas plants fed with different feedstocks compares to the current energy systems. Regarding the study on the economic assessment, Balaman and Selim (2014) de- veloped a supply chain model to determine the most appropriate loca- tion for the biogas plants in Izmir, considering the regional potential of different waste feedtocks. The plants had a payback period between four and eight years, similar to the findings (<10 years) in Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021). Akbulut (2012) re- ported that biogas-to-energy conversion from animal manure at a small-scale plant kWe) in the Agri province would be feasible, pay- ing back in 3.5 years and saving 21 % of GHG emissions compared to the uncontrolled disposal of manure. Estimating biogas potentials from an- imal manure across various regions of Turkey, Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021) indicated that 8 % of total electricity demand over the pe- riod 2007-2019 could have been supplied by AD of animal manure. Expanding the biogas potential analysis to include both animal manure and agricultural wastes, Aksay and Tabak (2022) suggested that 38 GWh (~10% of total) electricity can be generated annually through bio- gas conversion to electricity. The study also estimated that 174 Mt CO2 eq./yr (or 33 % of total national GHG emissions) could be reduced if the avoided emissions from coal electricity and traditional storage and management (i.e. land spreading) of animal manure were considered in the analysis. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

biogas energy supply by five times (to 1.5 EJ/yr) between 2000 and 2018 (IEA, 2020; REN21, 2019). This trend is expected to continue and reach up to ten times of today's level in 2050 as <10% of current biogas potential is utilised (IEA, 2020; IRENA, 2020).

With the development of energy and environmental policies supporting the use of renewables, there has also been a growing interest in the AD of agricultural residues for bioenergy in Turkey (MENR, 2014; MofEU, 2012; WMD, 2015). Being a party to the Paris Agreement, Turkey has set ambitious targets to reduce GHG emissions (by 21 % by 2030 compared to 2012; UNFCC, 2015) and increase the installed capac- ity of renewable energy (by 65 % by 2023), including 1000 MW from biomass (EMRA, 2018a; MENR, 2014, 2019). Considering Turkey's posi- tion in the global agricultural sector (7th largest agricultural producer in the world and 2nd largest chicken producer in Europe; INVEST, 2016), a huge amount of agricultural wastes is generated from farm activities and food wastes from the retail stage (Salihoglu et al., 2018). According to the latest estimates, around 180 Mt of animal manure is annually pro- duced in Turkey (GDRE, 2022), but <10% of it is converted into energy. In 2018, the country had 52 AD plants, with the total installed capacity of 95 MW, most of which were small-scale MWe) (GDRE, 2018). Similar to global trends (Bacenetti et al., 2016), animal manure is the most prevailing source used (either solely or combined with other feedstocks) in 70 of AD plants. Although energy crops are commonly used as feedstock in AD plants in Europe because of their high biogas yield (Ingrao et al., 2019), there are no plants using energy crops in Turkey yet. Furthermore, very little is currently known about the

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been widely used to evaluate the en- vironmental sustainability of biogas plants, with various studies reporting that energy generation from AD systems has lower GHG emis- sions as compared to fossil fuels (e.g. Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016). However, the performance of AD plants varies depending on many fac- tors, including feedstock type, operational parameters, biogas utilisation pathways and digestate treatment methods (Agostini et al., 2015), as well as the energy technologies displaced (Beausang et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019). All of these are also highly influenced by regional and spatial parameters, such as biomass availability, agricultural prac- tices and the infrastructure (Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016; Salvador et al., 2018). For example, in their review of LCA studies of 15 AD plants in Europe, Hijazi et al. (2016) found the plant scale, feedstock properties and regional factors as the most important parameters for biogas environmental sustainability. Geographical aspects were also found in the review by Aziz et al. (2019), which covered 48 papers, to have strong influence on LCA results, together with system boundaries. Moreover, Bacenetti et al. (2016), Rajendran and Murthy (2019) and Salvador et al. (2018) noted that most of LCA studies were based in Europe, so further studies are necessary to increase the understanding of the sustainability of biogas in other regions.
sustainability of energy from biogas in Turkey and to what degree it contributes to the national targets for GHG emissions reduction.

Along with the regional aspects, methodological choices, such as sys- tem boundaries, functional unit and databases, have critical importance for LCA results. For instance, adding a co-substrate, such as maize silage, to animal slurry usually improves the environmental sustainability of AD due to the higher biogas yield (Esteves et al., 2019). However, reducing the amount of manure going into the AD process by adding a co-substrate could reverse the results in comparison to the mono- digestion of manure since a lower amount of manure means lower benefits from the avoided impacts of manure storage (Lovarelli et al., 2019). Further, the biogas conversion efficiency benefits arising from adding a co-substrate can be offset due to longer feedstock-transport distances (Cusenza et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019) or indirect effects in sectors, such as competition with food and feed production, depend- ing on the substrate used (Cusenza et al., 2021). In this regard, if energy crops substitute animal manure, there is also a potential net increase in impacts due to their cultivation (Beausang et al., 2021) and possible land-use change impacts which are rarely addressed in literature (Esteves et al., 2019). For example, Zhang et al. (2021) found that co- digestion of pig manure with grass silage had lower life cycle impacts than its mono-digestion, while Beausang et al. (2021) revealed that the use of grass silage should be restricted when consequential LCA impacts from the intensification of grass production through additional nitrogen fertilisation were taken into account.

Limited number of studies evaluating life cycle economic implica- tions of AD plants is available, (Hamzehkolaei and Amjady, 2018; Lauer et al., 2017; Usack et al., 2017), as the majority has focused on ei- ther cost-benefit- or techno-economic analysis (Venanzi et al., 2018). These include net present value, payback time and internal rate of re- turn, in addition to electricity generation costs. Further, recent studies (Hamedani et al., 2020; Lovarelli et al., 2019; Zhang and Xu, 2020) have accounted for the monetary values of environmental impacts, par- ticularly GHG emission reduction costs (i.e. carbon credits), attempting to provide a more holistic view on the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants. A large difference in the electricity pro- duction costs via biogas ($25-$280/MWh.) was reported across different European countries (IEA/NEA, 2015), which in some cases can be higher than costs of fossil fuels (Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2014), depending on different parameters. For example, similar to re- sults found in Saracevic et al. (2019) for different biogas plants in Austria, Venanzi et al. (2018) found that operating and capital costs were the main reason for high costs of electricity from AD plants at a dairy farm in Italy. Lovarelli et al. (2019) and Hamedani et al. (2020) identified the economic profitability of a biogas plant in Italy was

related mainly to the electricity production and associated subsidies, in- cluding carbon credits; thus, larger plants were more profitable than the smaller. Similarly, in an economic analysis of biogas plants operated with food waste in the UK (Slorach et al., 2019a) and with organic household waste in Canada (Sanscartier et al., 2012), the plant scale was found to be an important factor as larger plants were more eco- nomic and financially viable compared to small-scale installations. However, it is still difficult to draw general conclusions on the sustain- ability of biogas plants as different approaches were taken in each study based on regional conditions (e.g., climate, farm practices, eco- nomic parameters), along with some methodological differences (e.g., by-product allocation and system boundaries).

In addition to the plant scale, the feedstock also has a significant ef- fect on the economic performance of biogas plants (Saracevic et al., 2019). For example, in a US study, higher organic loading of substrate in the co-digestion of animal manure was found economically better than manure-only digestion (Usack et al., 2017). However, as men- tioned above in the discussion on LCA, the use of energy crops as a co- substrate could counterbalance the environmental benefits as studies in Italy (Lovarelli et al., 2019) and Austria (Saracevic et al., 2019) dem- onstrated.

In Turkey, only one LCA study of biogas plants has been carried out (Nayal al., 2016), whereas several studies have focused on the eco- nomic assessment, either in a specific region (Akbulut, 2012; Balaman and Selim, 2014) or across the country (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil, 2021). Among these, Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Akbulut (2012) evaluated both the GHG savings and feasi- bility of electrity generation from biogas. The LCA study by Nayal et al. (2016) assessed the environmental sustainability of a small-scale (330 kWe) biogas plant using agricultural and farm wastes considering six indicators. The authors found that power derived from biogas had lower impacts compared to those from coal power plant; the digestate application was the main hotspot for the climate change impact and eu- trophication. However, it is unknown how the biogas plants fed with different feedstocks compares to the current energy systems. Regarding the study on the economic assessment, Balaman and Selim (2014) de- veloped a supply chain model to determine the most appropriate loca- tion for the biogas plants in Izmir, considering the regional potential of different waste feedtocks. The plants had a payback period between four and eight years, similar to the findings (<10 years) in Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021). Akbulut (2012) re- ported that biogas-to-energy conversion from animal manure at a small-scale plant kWe) in the Agri province would be feasible, pay- ing back in 3.5 years and saving 21 % of GHG emissions compared to the uncontrolled disposal of manure. Estimating biogas potentials from an- imal manure across various regions of Turkey, Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021) indicated that 8 % of total electricity demand over the pe- riod 2007-2019 could have been supplied by AD of animal manure. Expanding the biogas potential analysis to include both animal manure and agricultural wastes, Aksay and Tabak (2022) suggested that 38 GWh (~10% of total) electricity can be generated annually through bio- gas conversion to electricity. The study also estimated that 174 Mt CO2 eq./yr (or 33 % of total national GHG emissions) could be reduced if the avoided emissions from coal electricity and traditional storage and management (i.e. land spreading) of animal manure were considered in the analysis. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

(2021) revealed that the use of grass silage should be restricted when consequential LCA impacts from the intensification of grass production through additional nitrogen fertilisation were taken into account.

Limited number of studies evaluating life cycle economic implica- tions of AD plants is available, (Hamzehkolaei and Amjady, 2018; Lauer et al., 2017; Usack et al., 2017), as the majority has focused on ei- ther cost-benefit- or techno-economic analysis (Venanzi et al., 2018). These include net present value, payback time and internal rate of re- turn, in addition to electricity generation costs. Further, recent studies (Hamedani et al., 2020; Lovarelli et al., 2019; Zhang and Xu, 2020) have accounted for the monetary values of environmental impacts, par- ticularly GHG emission reduction costs (i.e. carbon credits), attempting to provide a more holistic view on the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants. A large difference in the electricity pro- duction costs via biogas ($25-$280/MWh.) was reported across different European countries (IEA/NEA, 2015), which in some cases can be higher than costs of fossil fuels (Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2014), depending on different parameters. For example, similar to re- sults found in Saracevic et al. (2019) for different biogas plants in Austria, Venanzi et al. (2018) found that operating and capital costs were the main reason for high costs of electricity from AD plants at a dairy farm in Italy. Lovarelli et al. (2019) and Hamedani et al. (2020) identified the economic profitability of a biogas plant in Italy was

related mainly to the electricity production and associated subsidies, in- cluding carbon credits; thus, larger plants were more profitable than the smaller. Similarly, in an economic analysis of biogas plants operated with food waste in the UK (Slorach et al., 2019a) and with organic household waste in Canada (Sanscartier et al., 2012), the plant scale was found to be an important factor as larger plants were more eco- nomic and financially viable compared to small-scale installations. However, it is still difficult to draw general conclusions on the sustain- ability of biogas plants as different approaches were taken in each study based on regional conditions (e.g., climate, farm practices, eco- nomic parameters), along with some methodological differences (e.g., by-product allocation and system boundaries).

In addition to the plant scale, the feedstock also has a significant ef- fect on the economic performance of biogas plants (Saracevic et al., 2019). For example, in a US study, higher organic loading of substrate in the co-digestion of animal manure was found economically better than manure-only digestion (Usack et al., 2017). However, as men- tioned above in the discussion on LCA, the use of energy crops as a co- substrate could counterbalance the environmental benefits as studies in Italy (Lovarelli et al., 2019) and Austria (Saracevic et al., 2019) dem- onstrated.

In Turkey, only one LCA study of biogas plants has been carried out (Nayal al., 2016), whereas several studies have focused on the eco- nomic assessment, either in a specific region (Akbulut, 2012; Balaman and Selim, 2014) or across the country (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil, 2021). Among these, Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Akbulut (2012) evaluated both the GHG savings and feasi- bility of electrity generation from biogas. The LCA study by Nayal et al. (2016) assessed the environmental sustainability of a small-scale (330 kWe) biogas plant using agricultural and farm wastes considering six indicators. The authors found that power derived from biogas had lower impacts compared to those from coal power plant; the digestate application was the main hotspot for the climate change impact and eu- trophication. However, it is unknown how the biogas plants fed with different feedstocks compares to the current energy systems. Regarding the study on the economic assessment, Balaman and Selim (2014) de- veloped a supply chain model to determine the most appropriate loca- tion for the biogas plants in Izmir, considering the regional potential of different waste feedtocks. The plants had a payback period between four and eight years, similar to the findings (<10 years) in Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021). Akbulut (2012) re- ported that biogas-to-energy conversion from animal manure at a small-scale plant kWe) in the Agri province would be feasible, pay- ing back in 3.5 years and saving 21 % of GHG emissions compared to the uncontrolled disposal of manure. Estimating biogas potentials from an- imal manure across various regions of Turkey, Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021) indicated that 8 % of total electricity demand over the pe- riod 2007-2019 could have been supplied by AD of animal manure. Expanding the biogas potential analysis to include both animal manure and agricultural wastes, Aksay and Tabak (2022) suggested that 38 GWh (~10% of total) electricity can be generated annually through bio- gas conversion to electricity. The study also estimated that 174 Mt CO2 eq./yr (or 33 % of total national GHG emissions) could be reduced if the avoided emissions from coal electricity and traditional storage and management (i.e. land spreading) of animal manure were considered in the analysis. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

biogas energy supply by five times (to 1.5 EJ/yr) between 2000 and 2018 (IEA, 2020; REN21, 2019). This trend is expected to continue and reach up to ten times of today's level in 2050 as <10% of current biogas potential is utilised (IEA, 2020; IRENA, 2020).

According to the latest estimates, around 180 Mt of animal manure is annually pro- duced in Turkey (GDRE, 2022), but <10% of it is converted into energy. In 2018, the country had 52 AD plants, with the total installed capacity of 95 MW, most of which were small-scale MWe) (GDRE, 2018). Similar to global trends (Bacenetti et al., 2016), animal manure is the most prevailing source used (either solely or combined with other feedstocks) in 70 of AD plants. Although energy crops are commonly used as feedstock in AD plants in Europe because of their high biogas yield (Ingrao et al., 2019), there are no plants using energy crops in Turkey yet. (2016), Rajendran and Murthy (2019) and Salvador et al. sustainability of energy from biogas in Turkey and to what degree it contributes to the national targets for GHG emissions reduction.

It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

biogas energy supply by five times (to 1.5 EJ/yr) between 2000 and 2018 (IEA, 2020; REN21, 2019). This trend is expected to continue and reach up to ten times of today's level in 2050 as <10% of current biogas potential is utilised (IEA, 2020; IRENA, 2020).

With the development of energy and environmental policies supporting the use of renewables, there has also been a growing interest in the AD of agricultural residues for bioenergy in Turkey (MENR, 2014; MofEU, 2012; WMD, 2015). Being a party to the Paris Agreement, Turkey has set ambitious targets to reduce GHG emissions (by 21 % by 2030 compared to 2012; UNFCC, 2015) and increase the installed capac- ity of renewable energy (by 65 % by 2023), including 1000 MW from biomass (EMRA, 2018a; MENR, 2014, 2019). Considering Turkey's posi- tion in the global agricultural sector (7th largest agricultural producer in the world and 2nd largest chicken producer in Europe; INVEST, 2016), a huge amount of agricultural wastes is generated from farm activities and food wastes from the retail stage (Salihoglu et al., 2018). According to the latest estimates, around 180 Mt of animal manure is annually pro- duced in Turkey (GDRE, 2022), but <10% of it is converted into energy. In 2018, the country had 52 AD plants, with the total installed capacity of 95 MW, most of which were small-scale MWe) (GDRE, 2018). Similar to global trends (Bacenetti et al., 2016), animal manure is the most prevailing source used (either solely or combined with other feedstocks) in 70 of AD plants. Although energy crops are commonly used as feedstock in AD plants in Europe because of their high biogas yield (Ingrao et al., 2019), there are no plants using energy crops in Turkey yet. Furthermore, very little is currently known about the

Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been widely used to evaluate the en- vironmental sustainability of biogas plants, with various studies reporting that energy generation from AD systems has lower GHG emis- sions as compared to fossil fuels (e.g. Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016). However, the performance of AD plants varies depending on many fac- tors, including feedstock type, operational parameters, biogas utilisation pathways and digestate treatment methods (Agostini et al., 2015), as well as the energy technologies displaced (Beausang et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019). All of these are also highly influenced by regional and spatial parameters, such as biomass availability, agricultural prac- tices and the infrastructure (Aziz et al., 2019; Hijazi et al., 2016; Salvador et al., 2018). For example, in their review of LCA studies of 15 AD plants in Europe, Hijazi et al. (2016) found the plant scale, feedstock properties and regional factors as the most important parameters for biogas environmental sustainability. Geographical aspects were also found in the review by Aziz et al. (2019), which covered 48 papers, to have strong influence on LCA results, together with system boundaries. Moreover, Bacenetti et al. (2016), Rajendran and Murthy (2019) and Salvador et al. (2018) noted that most of LCA studies were based in Europe, so further studies are necessary to increase the understanding of the sustainability of biogas in other regions.
sustainability of energy from biogas in Turkey and to what degree it contributes to the national targets for GHG emissions reduction.

Along with the regional aspects, methodological choices, such as sys- tem boundaries, functional unit and databases, have critical importance for LCA results. For instance, adding a co-substrate, such as maize silage, to animal slurry usually improves the environmental sustainability of AD due to the higher biogas yield (Esteves et al., 2019). However, reducing the amount of manure going into the AD process by adding a co-substrate could reverse the results in comparison to the mono- digestion of manure since a lower amount of manure means lower benefits from the avoided impacts of manure storage (Lovarelli et al., 2019). Further, the biogas conversion efficiency benefits arising from adding a co-substrate can be offset due to longer feedstock-transport distances (Cusenza et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019) or indirect effects in sectors, such as competition with food and feed production, depend- ing on the substrate used (Cusenza et al., 2021). In this regard, if energy crops substitute animal manure, there is also a potential net increase in impacts due to their cultivation (Beausang et al., 2021) and possible land-use change impacts which are rarely addressed in literature (Esteves et al., 2019). For example, Zhang et al. (2021) found that co- digestion of pig manure with grass silage had lower life cycle impacts than its mono-digestion, while Beausang et al. (2021) revealed that the use of grass silage should be restricted when consequential LCA impacts from the intensification of grass production through additional nitrogen fertilisation were taken into account.

Limited number of studies evaluating life cycle economic implica- tions of AD plants is available, (Hamzehkolaei and Amjady, 2018; Lauer et al., 2017; Usack et al., 2017), as the majority has focused on ei- ther cost-benefit- or techno-economic analysis (Venanzi et al., 2018). These include net present value, payback time and internal rate of re- turn, in addition to electricity generation costs. Further, recent studies (Hamedani et al., 2020; Lovarelli et al., 2019; Zhang and Xu, 2020) have accounted for the monetary values of environmental impacts, par- ticularly GHG emission reduction costs (i.e. carbon credits), attempting to provide a more holistic view on the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants. A large difference in the electricity pro- duction costs via biogas ($25-$280/MWh.) was reported across different European countries (IEA/NEA, 2015), which in some cases can be higher than costs of fossil fuels (Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2014), depending on different parameters. For example, similar to re- sults found in Saracevic et al. (2019) for different biogas plants in Austria, Venanzi et al. (2018) found that operating and capital costs were the main reason for high costs of electricity from AD plants at a dairy farm in Italy. Lovarelli et al. (2019) and Hamedani et al. (2020) identified the economic profitability of a biogas plant in Italy was

related mainly to the electricity production and associated subsidies, in- cluding carbon credits; thus, larger plants were more profitable than the smaller. Similarly, in an economic analysis of biogas plants operated with food waste in the UK (Slorach et al., 2019a) and with organic household waste in Canada (Sanscartier et al., 2012), the plant scale was found to be an important factor as larger plants were more eco- nomic and financially viable compared to small-scale installations. However, it is still difficult to draw general conclusions on the sustain- ability of biogas plants as different approaches were taken in each study based on regional conditions (e.g., climate, farm practices, eco- nomic parameters), along with some methodological differences (e.g., by-product allocation and system boundaries).

In addition to the plant scale, the feedstock also has a significant ef- fect on the economic performance of biogas plants (Saracevic et al., 2019). For example, in a US study, higher organic loading of substrate in the co-digestion of animal manure was found economically better than manure-only digestion (Usack et al., 2017). However, as men- tioned above in the discussion on LCA, the use of energy crops as a co- substrate could counterbalance the environmental benefits as studies in Italy (Lovarelli et al., 2019) and Austria (Saracevic et al., 2019) dem- onstrated.

In Turkey, only one LCA study of biogas plants has been carried out (Nayal al., 2016), whereas several studies have focused on the eco- nomic assessment, either in a specific region (Akbulut, 2012; Balaman and Selim, 2014) or across the country (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil, 2021). Among these, Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Akbulut (2012) evaluated both the GHG savings and feasi- bility of electrity generation from biogas. The LCA study by Nayal et al. (2016) assessed the environmental sustainability of a small-scale (330 kWe) biogas plant using agricultural and farm wastes considering six indicators. The authors found that power derived from biogas had lower impacts compared to those from coal power plant; the digestate application was the main hotspot for the climate change impact and eu- trophication. However, it is unknown how the biogas plants fed with different feedstocks compares to the current energy systems. Regarding the study on the economic assessment, Balaman and Selim (2014) de- veloped a supply chain model to determine the most appropriate loca- tion for the biogas plants in Izmir, considering the regional potential of different waste feedtocks. The plants had a payback period between four and eight years, similar to the findings (<10 years) in Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021). Akbulut (2012) re- ported that biogas-to-energy conversion from animal manure at a small-scale plant kWe) in the Agri province would be feasible, pay- ing back in 3.5 years and saving 21 % of GHG emissions compared to the uncontrolled disposal of manure. Estimating biogas potentials from an- imal manure across various regions of Turkey, Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021) indicated that 8 % of total electricity demand over the pe- riod 2007-2019 could have been supplied by AD of animal manure. Expanding the biogas potential analysis to include both animal manure and agricultural wastes, Aksay and Tabak (2022) suggested that 38 GWh (~10% of total) electricity can be generated annually through bio- gas conversion to electricity. The study also estimated that 174 Mt CO2 eq./yr (or 33 % of total national GHG emissions) could be reduced if the avoided emissions from coal electricity and traditional storage and management (i.e. land spreading) of animal manure were considered in the analysis. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

However, reducing the amount of manure going into the AD process by adding a co-substrate could reverse the results in comparison to the mono- digestion of manure since a lower amount of manure means lower benefits from the avoided impacts of manure storage (Lovarelli et al., 2019). Further, the biogas conversion efficiency benefits arising from adding a co-substrate can be offset due to longer feedstock-transport distances (Cusenza et al., 2021; Esteves et al., 2019) or indirect effects in sectors, such as competition with food and feed production, depend- ing on the substrate used (Cusenza et al., 2021). In this regard, if energy crops substitute animal manure, there is also a potential net increase in impacts due to their cultivation (Beausang et al., 2021) and possible land-use change impacts which are rarely addressed in literature (Esteves et al., 2019). For example, Zhang et al. (2021) found that co- digestion of pig manure with grass silage had lower life cycle impacts than its mono-digestion, while Beausang et al. (2021) revealed that the use of grass silage should be restricted when consequential LCA impacts from the intensification of grass production through additional nitrogen fertilisation were taken into account.

Limited number of studies evaluating life cycle economic implica- tions of AD plants is available, (Hamzehkolaei and Amjady, 2018; Lauer et al., 2017; Usack et al., 2017), as the majority has focused on ei- ther cost-benefit- or techno-economic analysis (Venanzi et al., 2018). These include net present value, payback time and internal rate of re- turn, in addition to electricity generation costs. Further, recent studies (Hamedani et al., 2020; Lovarelli et al., 2019; Zhang and Xu, 2020) have accounted for the monetary values of environmental impacts, par- ticularly GHG emission reduction costs (i.e. carbon credits), attempting to provide a more holistic view on the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants. A large difference in the electricity pro- duction costs via biogas ($25-$280/MWh.) was reported across different European countries (IEA/NEA, 2015), which in some cases can be higher than costs of fossil fuels (Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2014), depending on different parameters. For example, similar to re- sults found in Saracevic et al. (2019) for different biogas plants in Austria, Venanzi et al. (2018) found that operating and capital costs were the main reason for high costs of electricity from AD plants at a dairy farm in Italy. Lovarelli et al. (2019) and Hamedani et al. (2020) identified the economic profitability of a biogas plant in Italy was

related mainly to the electricity production and associated subsidies, in- cluding carbon credits; thus, larger plants were more profitable than the smaller. Similarly, in an economic analysis of biogas plants operated with food waste in the UK (Slorach et al., 2019a) and with organic household waste in Canada (Sanscartier et al., 2012), the plant scale was found to be an important factor as larger plants were more eco- nomic and financially viable compared to small-scale installations. However, it is still difficult to draw general conclusions on the sustain- ability of biogas plants as different approaches were taken in each study based on regional conditions (e.g., climate, farm practices, eco- nomic parameters), along with some methodological differences (e.g., by-product allocation and system boundaries).

In addition to the plant scale, the feedstock also has a significant ef- fect on the economic performance of biogas plants (Saracevic et al., 2019). For example, in a US study, higher organic loading of substrate in the co-digestion of animal manure was found economically better than manure-only digestion (Usack et al., 2017). However, as men- tioned above in the discussion on LCA, the use of energy crops as a co- substrate could counterbalance the environmental benefits as studies in Italy (Lovarelli et al., 2019) and Austria (Saracevic et al., 2019) dem- onstrated.

In Turkey, only one LCA study of biogas plants has been carried out (Nayal al., 2016), whereas several studies have focused on the eco- nomic assessment, either in a specific region (Akbulut, 2012; Balaman and Selim, 2014) or across the country (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil, 2021). Among these, Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Akbulut (2012) evaluated both the GHG savings and feasi- bility of electrity generation from biogas. The LCA study by Nayal et al. (2016) assessed the environmental sustainability of a small-scale (330 kWe) biogas plant using agricultural and farm wastes considering six indicators. The authors found that power derived from biogas had lower impacts compared to those from coal power plant; the digestate application was the main hotspot for the climate change impact and eu- trophication. However, it is unknown how the biogas plants fed with different feedstocks compares to the current energy systems. Regarding the study on the economic assessment, Balaman and Selim (2014) de- veloped a supply chain model to determine the most appropriate loca- tion for the biogas plants in Izmir, considering the regional potential of different waste feedtocks. The plants had a payback period between four and eight years, similar to the findings (<10 years) in Aksay and Tabak (2022) and Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021). Akbulut (2012) re- ported that biogas-to-energy conversion from animal manure at a small-scale plant kWe) in the Agri province would be feasible, pay- ing back in 3.5 years and saving 21 % of GHG emissions compared to the uncontrolled disposal of manure. Estimating biogas potentials from an- imal manure across various regions of Turkey, Caliskan and Tumen Ozdil (2021) indicated that 8 % of total electricity demand over the pe- riod 2007-2019 could have been supplied by AD of animal manure. Expanding the biogas potential analysis to include both animal manure and agricultural wastes, Aksay and Tabak (2022) suggested that 38 GWh (~10% of total) electricity can be generated annually through bio- gas conversion to electricity. The study also estimated that 174 Mt CO2 eq./yr (or 33 % of total national GHG emissions) could be reduced if the avoided emissions from coal electricity and traditional storage and management (i.e. land spreading) of animal manure were considered in the analysis. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.

biogas energy supply by five times (to 1.5 EJ/yr) between 2000 and 2018 (IEA, 2020; REN21, 2019). This trend is expected to continue and reach up to ten times of today's level in 2050 as <10% of current biogas potential is utilised (IEA, 2020; IRENA, 2020).

According to the latest estimates, around 180 Mt of animal manure is annually pro- duced in Turkey (GDRE, 2022), but <10% of it is converted into energy. In 2018, the country had 52 AD plants, with the total installed capacity of 95 MW, most of which were small-scale MWe) (GDRE, 2018). Similar to global trends (Bacenetti et al., 2016), animal manure is the most prevailing source used (either solely or combined with other feedstocks) in 70 of AD plants. Although energy crops are commonly used as feedstock in AD plants in Europe because of their high biogas yield (Ingrao et al., 2019), there are no plants using energy crops in Turkey yet. However, the economic analysis in these studies only fo- cused on the profitability of the system and only GHG emissions from the digester and cogeneration of heat and power (CHP) plant were con- sidered, rather than the whole life cycle, as well as excluding other en- vironmental impacts. Further, none of the previous studies assessed the economic sustainability of AD plants from a life cycle perspective and the utilisation of energy crops as a co-substrate for biogas plants in Turkey.

Considering the previous studies, which are restricted to certain re- gions and impacts and focused on single energy plants or single biomass resources in Turkey, this paper sets out to address these research gaps

through an evaluation of the environmental and economic sustainabil- ity of heat and electricity derived from biogas in Turkey. It considers life cycle environmental and economic impacts of differing biogas plans fed with different waste feedstocks and an energy crop and as- sesses their relative performance to the current energy systems, i.e. electricity from the grid and heat from natural gas. The novelties of this study include:

<list>an assessment of life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of four biogas plant utilising six different feedstocks, including animal and vegetable wastes and maize silage as an energy crop to generate heat and electricity in Turkey; 
a sustainability assessment of the above biogas-to-energy systems considering a comprehensive set of sustainability indicators with 17 environmental impacts and four economic indicators; 
a comparison of the life cycle impacts and costs to the current energy sources in Turkey; and 
an evaluation of the environmental impacts and economic implica- tions of biogas systems at the national level.</list>

The outcomes of this study will be useful for policy makers and plant operators helping them understand environmental and economic sus- tainability of energy from biogas and make more informed decisions to- wards sustainable development of bioenergy in Turkey.
16: The environmental sustainability has been assessed via LCA, in ac- cordance with the LCA methodology in the ISO 14040/44 Standards (ISO, 2006a, 2006b). The energy systems have been modelled in GaBi v8.7 (Thinkstep, 2019) and the environmental impacts estimated using the ReCiPe 2016 midpoint method (Huijbregts et al., 2017). The economic sustainability has been evaluated by estimating the life cycle costs (LCC), net present value (NPV) and payback time (PB). LCC have been determined based on the approach suggested by Hunkeler et al. (2008) and Swarr et al. (2011). The methods and inventory data used in the assessment of the environmental and economic sustainability are detailed below, following the definition of the goal and scope of the study outlined next.
17: The main goal of this study is to evaluate the life cycle environmental and economic sustainability of heat and electricity co-generated from biogas in Turkey, through utilisation of various feedstocks in biogas plants. The specific objectives are as follows:

<list>to evaluate the environmental and economic sustainability of heat and electricity from biogas relative to the current sources of heat (nat- ural gas) and electricity (grid) in Turkey; 
to determine the main life cycle stages contributing to the environ- mental impacts; 
to determine the total costs of energy generation at the plant level and evaluate the economic feasibility of biogas installations; and 
to determine the potential contribution of biogas to reducing the na- tional GHG emissions and costs of fossil-fuel imports.</list>
As outlined in Fig. 1, the scope of the study is from cradle to grave. As can also be seen in the figure, the biogas plants under study use the fol- lowing combinations of feedstocks:

<list>i. Plant A: mixture of cattle and chicken manure (both in solid form); 
ii. Plant B: mixture of slaughterhouse wastes, vegetable wastes, cattle slurry and chicken manure; 
iii. Plant C: cattle slurry; and 
iv. Plant D: mixture of maize silage and cattle slurry.</list>
Plant A|Plant B|Plant C|Plant D Cattle manure|Cattle slurry|Cattle slurry|Cattle slurry Chicken manure|Chicken manure Vegetable wastes Slaughterhouse wastes||Maize silage

Fig. 1. System boundaries for bioenergy generation via anaerobic digestion of various feedstocks. [T: Transport. The organic loading rates for Plants A, B, C and D are 95 t/d (63% cattle and 37% chicken manure), 125 t/d (60% cattle slurry, 23 vegetable wastes, 9% chicken manure and slaughterhouse wastes), 250 t/d and 215 t/d (81 cattle slurry, 19% maize silage), respectively].

These feedstocks and their combinations have been selected based on their availability in Turkey, as discussed in the Introduction. The system is divided into the following sub-systems (Fig. 1): feed- stock cultivation (Plant D), collection, transport and pre-treatment at the plant; plant construction; plant operation (biogas production in the AD plant and biogas combustion in the CHP unit to co-produce heat and electricity); digestate storage; and digestate application on ag- ricultural land. The system is credited for the production of digestate and the resulting avoidance of chemical fertilisers. These stages are de- scribed in more detail in the inventory data section.
The functional unit is defined as '1 MWh of net electricity and heat co- generated in a biogas plant'.

20: Primary data for Plants A and B have been collected from the envi- ronmental impact assessment reports of two proposed AD plants in Turkey (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017). Plants C and D are designed accord- ing to the technical data provided in Dereli et al. (2012). The majority of the background data are from the Ecoinvent v3.3 database (Ecoinvent, 2016) which have been modified for Turkish conditions where relevant, including the country specific grid mix and crop cultivation. Further in- formation on the data is provided below.
21: It is assumed that animal manure would be collected on a daily basis from nearby dairy and chicken farms located within a radius of 20 km from the plant. Crop silage is also assumed to be obtained from nearby farms within a radius of 5 km. Vegetable and slaughterhouse wastes are collected separately from their sources and transferred to municipal
solid waste transfer stations. From there, they are then transported to the plant by trucks for 25 km and 10 km, respectively.

The upstream emissions from waste feedstock generation (i.e. fossil CO2 and all other emissions) are excluded as the waste is considered burden-free (the cut-off approach). However, they are considered for the production of maize silage (used as co-substrate in Plant D). This has been modelled by modifying the Ecoinvent dataset to correspond to the average data for Turkey (as explained in Section S1.1 in the Sup- plementary Information (SI)). It is also assumed that emissions from the feedstock storage are negligible as they are stored for a short time (<2-3 days) in covered storage tanks (Hamelin et al., 2010). Properties of feedstocks used in each plant are given in Table 1 which have been obtained from the primary reports (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017) and pre- vious studies (Dereli et al., 2012), unless stated otherwise. It is also im- portant to note that the properties of cattle manure (solid or slurry) are different depending on its total solid content. As the data have been col- lected from the separate reports and studies, feedstocks may differ in their characteristics even though they are categorised under the same name.

To improve the digestibility of substrates, all feedstocks undergo mechanical pre-treatment (i.e., size comminution). After this, the feed stream is mixed homogeneously with digestate and heated up to the operating temperature. Further details are provided in the next section.

2.2.2. Anaerobic digestion and combined heat and power (CHP) plants Anaerobic digestion is a biochemical process in which micro- organisms break down the organic matter in the absence of oxygen (Baldi et al., 2019). Table 2 summarises the technical properties of the biogas plants studied, with the inventory data provided in Table 3.

a Calorific value and density of methane are 9.9 kWh/m³ and 0.65 kg/m³. b Source: EnBa (2017). C Source: DiMuh (2017). d Source: Dereli et al. (2012).

These plants have been selected considering the potential feedstocks for biogas production, trends in their use (DBFZ, 2011; MofEU/TUBITAK, 2018), as well as the availability of data. As cattle manure is the most abundant but underutilised resource, a plant using only this feed- stock has been modelled, which could be the case for animal farms because of the ease of operation. Maize silage is chosen as an energy crop for co-digestion with animal manure to generate biogas due to its wide cultivation and large production capacity across Turkey (TUIK, 2018a). The selection of plant capacities is based on the previ- ous literature on the feasibility of biogas plants in Turkey, as men- tioned in the Introduction.

All the biogas plants considered here operate under mesophilic con- ditions (35-40 °C) with either single- or two-stage processes with the retention time of 30-40 days. In the pre-treatment stage, the feedstocks are shredded, mixed and diluted by recycling liquid digestate (3-4% total solids, TS) to ensure 8-15 % of TS (Dereli et al., 2012; DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017).

The biogas plants are considered to be energetically self-sufficient, assuming that the parasitic heat and electricity demand is supplied by the energy produced by the plants. The parasitic heat and electricity

consumption are either calculated based on the primary data or ob- tained from literature (see Tables 2 and 3). Heating the feed stream to the digestion temperature is the most energy intensive process, using 20-28% of gross heat produced in plant. A portion (10%) of the total heat generated is also required to maintain the reaction temperature and compensate heat losses from the digester through side walls, floor and tops. These have been calculated according to the models used in previous studies (Dereli et al., 2012; Kocar et al., 2016; Yangin-Gomeo and Ozturk, 2013). Apart from heat, the parasitic electricity consump- tion across the whole biogas plant (pumping, shredding, conveying and mixing) ranges from 8 % to 15 % of gross electrical capacity (Fusi et al., 2016; Lijó et al., 2014). Here, this is assumed to be in the range of 10-13 (see Table 3).

The biogas is fed to the CHP unit to generate heat and electricity after removing H2S by air-dosing into gas holder. Reciprocating gas engine is considered in this study for energy recovery as it is the most common type of engine used in biogas plants in Turkey with a high gross power-to-heat (P/H) ratio (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017). It is sized based on the lower heating value of biogas (see Table 3) and taking 0.98 as the average gross P/H ratio. Considering the net output from

These have been calculated according to the models used in previous studies (Dereli et al., 2012; Kocar et al., 2016; Yangin-Gomeo and Ozturk, 2013). Apart from heat, the parasitic electricity consump- tion across the whole biogas plant (pumping, shredding, conveying and mixing) ranges from 8 % to 15 % of gross electrical capacity (Fusi et al., 2016; Lijó et al., 2014). Here, this is assumed to be in the range of 10-13 (see Table 3).

The biogas is fed to the CHP unit to generate heat and electricity after removing H2S by air-dosing into gas holder. Reciprocating gas engine is considered in this study for energy recovery as it is the most common type of engine used in biogas plants in Turkey with a high gross power-to-heat (P/H) ratio (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017). It is sized based on the lower heating value of biogas (see Table 3) and taking 0.98 as the average gross P/H ratio. Considering the net output from

24: It is assumed that animal manure would be collected on a daily basis from nearby dairy and chicken farms located within a radius of 20 km from the plant. Crop silage is also assumed to be obtained from nearby farms within a radius of 5 km. Vegetable and slaughterhouse wastes are collected separately from their sources and transferred to municipal
solid waste transfer stations. From there, they are then transported to the plant by trucks for 25 km and 10 km, respectively.

The upstream emissions from waste feedstock generation (i.e. fossil CO2 and all other emissions) are excluded as the waste is considered burden-free (the cut-off approach). However, they are considered for the production of maize silage (used as co-substrate in Plant D). This has been modelled by modifying the Ecoinvent dataset to correspond to the average data for Turkey (as explained in Section S1.1 in the Sup- plementary Information (SI)). It is also assumed that emissions from the feedstock storage are negligible as they are stored for a short time (<2-3 days) in covered storage tanks (Hamelin et al., 2010). Properties of feedstocks used in each plant are given in Table 1 which have been obtained from the primary reports (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017) and pre- vious studies (Dereli et al., 2012), unless stated otherwise. It is also im- portant to note that the properties of cattle manure (solid or slurry) are different depending on its total solid content. As the data have been col- lected from the separate reports and studies, feedstocks may differ in their characteristics even though they are categorised under the same name.

To improve the digestibility of substrates, all feedstocks undergo mechanical pre-treatment (i.e., size comminution). After this, the feed stream is mixed homogeneously with digestate and heated up to the operating temperature. Further details are provided in the next section.

These plants have been selected considering the potential feedstocks for biogas production, trends in their use (DBFZ, 2011; MofEU/TUBITAK, 2018), as well as the availability of data. As cattle manure is the most abundant but underutilised resource, a plant using only this feed- stock has been modelled, which could be the case for animal farms because of the ease of operation. Maize silage is chosen as an energy crop for co-digestion with animal manure to generate biogas due to its wide cultivation and large production capacity across Turkey (TUIK, 2018a). All the biogas plants considered here operate under mesophilic con- ditions (35-40 °C) with either single- or two-stage processes with the retention time of 30-40 days. In the pre-treatment stage, the feedstocks are shredded, mixed and diluted by recycling liquid digestate (3-4% total solids, TS) to ensure 8-15 % of TS (Dereli et al., 2012; DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017).

The biogas plants are considered to be energetically self-sufficient, assuming that the parasitic heat and electricity demand is supplied by the energy produced by the plants. The parasitic heat and electricity

consumption are either calculated based on the primary data or ob- tained from literature (see Tables 2 and 3). Heating the feed stream to the digestion temperature is the most energy intensive process, using 20-28% of gross heat produced in plant. A portion (10%) of the total heat generated is also required to maintain the reaction temperature and compensate heat losses from the digester through side walls, floor and tops. These have been calculated according to the models used in previous studies (Dereli et al., 2012; Kocar et al., 2016; Yangin-Gomeo and Ozturk, 2013). Apart from heat, the parasitic electricity consump- tion across the whole biogas plant (pumping, shredding, conveying and mixing) ranges from 8 % to 15 % of gross electrical capacity (Fusi et al., 2016; Lijó et al., 2014). Here, this is assumed to be in the range of 10-13 (see Table 3).

The biogas is fed to the CHP unit to generate heat and electricity after removing H2S by air-dosing into gas holder. Reciprocating gas engine is considered in this study for energy recovery as it is the most common type of engine used in biogas plants in Turkey with a high gross power-to-heat (P/H) ratio (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017). It is sized based on the lower heating value of biogas (see Table 3) and taking 0.98 as the average gross P/H ratio. Considering the net output from

28: It is assumed that animal manure would be collected on a daily basis from nearby dairy and chicken farms located within a radius of 20 km from the plant. Crop silage is also assumed to be obtained from nearby farms within a radius of 5 km. Vegetable and slaughterhouse wastes are collected separately from their sources and transferred to municipal
solid waste transfer stations. From there, they are then transported to the plant by trucks for 25 km and 10 km, respectively.

The upstream emissions from waste feedstock generation (i.e. fossil CO2 and all other emissions) are excluded as the waste is considered burden-free (the cut-off approach). However, they are considered for the production of maize silage (used as co-substrate in Plant D). This has been modelled by modifying the Ecoinvent dataset to correspond to the average data for Turkey (as explained in Section S1.1 in the Sup- plementary Information (SI)). It is also assumed that emissions from the feedstock storage are negligible as they are stored for a short time (<2-3 days) in covered storage tanks (Hamelin et al., 2010). Properties of feedstocks used in each plant are given in Table 1 which have been obtained from the primary reports (DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017) and pre- vious studies (Dereli et al., 2012), unless stated otherwise. It is also im- portant to note that the properties of cattle manure (solid or slurry) are different depending on its total solid content. As the data have been col- lected from the separate reports and studies, feedstocks may differ in their characteristics even though they are categorised under the same name.

To improve the digestibility of substrates, all feedstocks undergo mechanical pre-treatment (i.e., size comminution). After this, the feed stream is mixed homogeneously with digestate and heated up to the operating temperature. Further details are provided in the next section.
