36: Raw digestate in this study contains 5-9 % of TS (Dereli et al., 2012; DiMuh, 2017; EnBa, 2017), and it is processed either through decanter centrifuge or screw press to separate solid and liquid fractions. Moreover, the liquid fraction is treated at 70 °C for 1 h to eliminate pathogens and other harmful organisms, and then stored in closed tanks according to Turkish regulations. The elemental composition of digestate is strongly dependent on the feedstocks, AD process conditions (Poeschl et al., 2012) and the separation tech- nology (Di Maria et al., 2016; Drosg et al., 2015). Table S2 shows the
nutrients content of the digestate calculated in this study (for details, see Section S1.3 in SI).

Separated liquid digestate is assumed to be stored in big lagoons and the emissions of ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxides (NOX) from storage are calculated using the factors reported in Stucki et al. (2011). Both solid and liquid digestates are assumed to be transported to nearby fields to a distance of 5 and 10 km (Fantin et al., 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012; Stucki et al., 2011), respectively. The system is credited for avoiding chemical fertilisers accordingly. Oxides of nitrogen and NH3 released during digestate application are computed using data in Table 3 and emission factors from literature as detailed in Table S3 in SI. Phosphorous leaching is estimated using the equation provided in Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011). Potassium leaching is not considered here as it has a negligible effect in terms of environmental impacts (Tonini et al., 2012).

The amount of chemical fertilisers displaced by digestate is calcu- lated considering the plant availability of each nutrient within digestate. The availability of nitrogen to plants from the digestate is estimated to be in the range of 45-65%, and lower for animal-manure based digestate than other feedstocks (Nguyen and Hermansen, 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012; Rigby and Smith, 2011). Phosphorous and potassium nutrients are assumed to be fully available (Lijó et al., 2014; Nguyen and Hermansen, 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012). The types of chemical fertilisers substituted from using digestate are defined based on the latest chemi- cal fertiliser consumption in Turkey (IFA, 2016). All background data re- garding fertiliser production and transportation are sourced from Ecoinvent (2016). For a full list of inorganic fertilisers displaced, see Table S4 in SI. Each of field emissions (NH3, NO, NOx, N20, CO2 and PO4) from chemical fertiliser application are calculated according to models provided in Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011), considering the amount and types of the fertiliser applied.

39: The following 17 impact categories included in the ReCiPe 2016 mid- point (H) method (Huijbregts et al., 2017) are considered the environ- mental sustainability assessment via LCA:

<list>climate change: global warming potential (GWP), excluding biogenic CO2 emissions; 
freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication and terrestrial acid- ification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP, respectively); 
ozone depletion potential (ODP); 
agricultural land occupation (LU) and water depletion (WD) potentials; 
particulate matter formation potential (PMF); 
photo-chemical oxidant formation potentials affecting ecosystems and human health (POF-Eco and POF-Hum); 
fossil fuel and mineral depletion potentials (FD and MD); and 
human toxicity and ecotoxicity: human toxicity cancer/non-cancer po- tentials c,HT-nc), freshwater, marine and terrestrial ecotoxicity po- tentials (FET, MET and TET, respectively).</list>
40: In line with the LCA, the economic assessment takes into account all construction and operating costs, as well as the additional benefits in the supply chain over the lifetime of biogas plants. These benefits in- clude revenues from the heat, electricity and digestate, as well as the premium feed-in-tariff (FIT) for biopower and incentives for the plant equipment (REL, 2010).
To estimate the LCC, discounted cash flow is considered over the life- time of the project as follows:

LCC|life cycle costs of heat and electricity from biogas ($/MWhe+th) LCC1|net life cycle costs of heat and electricity from biogas ($/lifetime) S|net heat and electricity generated in biogas plant (MWhe+th/lifetime) Cc|overnight capital costs ($/lifetime) CO&M,n|non-feed operating and maintenance costs in year n ($/yr) CB,n|cost of biomass production or collection and transport in year n ($/yr) Csv,n|revenues from digestate in year n ($/yr) C1,n|incentives in year n ($/yr) SEn,n|net heat and electricity generated in year n (MWhe+th/yr) i|discount rate (%) t|total lifetime of biogas plant (years).

Furthermore, levelised costs of electricity (LCOE) and levelised costs of heat (LCOH) are calculated considering only installation and operating costs (IEA/NEA, 2015) and costs are allocated between electricity and heat according to their exergy values (for details, see Table S5 in SI).

The economic feasibility of the project is also investigated via NPV and PB methods as follows: (2) where: NPV net present value of biogas plant over its lifetime ($/lifetime) Rtot,n total income generated by biogas plant in year n ($/lifetime).

RET income from the sales of power for export-tariff in year n ($/yr) RFIT income from the sales of power at additional FIT in year n ($/yr) RES income from the sales of power for additional incentives for plant equipment in year n ($/yr) Rh income from the sales of heat in year n ($/yr) Rt income from the sales of digestate in year n ($/yr).

The payback is calculated based on the discounted values of cash-ins and cash-outs (discounted payback period) and determined once the NPV changes its sign from negative to positive. It is formulated as fol- lows (Lamidi et al., 2019):

PB payback period of biogas plant (years) Apci the last period with negative cumulative discounted cash-ins (years) BDCI the absolute value of cumulative discounted cash-ins at the end of period A ($/yr) CDCI discounted cash-ins after period A ($/yr).

The costs are estimated using 2018 as the base year and a 10% % dis- count rate. The latter is based on the average interest rate provided by Central Bank of Turkey over last ten years (TCMB, 2018). This value was also suggested for bioenergy projects (FAO, 2016) and used in pre- vious assessments (Akbulut, 2012; Ates, 2015) and energy projections in Turkey (Atilgan and Azapagic, 2016). All costs are expressed in US$, using the average exchange rate of 1 US$ = 4.8 TRY (Turkish lira) in 2018 (TCMB, 2018). The following sections provide an overview of the cost inventory data which are primarily sourced from Turkey and summarised in Table 4.

It is assumed that animal manure and other wastes are freely available for collection. The production cost of maize silage in Turkey is estimated using data from Karaagac et al. (2016) and Tuvanc and Dagdemir (2009). The collection costs of wet agricultural residues and other organic wastes from slaughterhouse and bazaars are estimated based on $0.62/km of transport cost via 16 t truck (BAKKA, 2019; Kaya et al., 2015). Following these, the final costs of feedstock transport vary between $1.25/t and $2.27/t, depending on the number of journeys, distance and daily biomass required for each plant.

As biogas is a well-established technology in Turkey, the initial cap- ital investment cost is selected based on the establishment cost of biogas plants in various country's regions (see Table S6 in SI). Given the effect of time on the construction of the plant, the older cost data are revised according to the Chemical Engineering Plant Cost Index (CEPCI) (ChemEngOnline, 2018; Turton et al., 2018). This has resulted in $2171/kWe capacity (or $75/t of waste processed annually) as the average of the available data. Fixed annual maintenance cost of AD plant is assumed to be 2 % of capital cost (Lauer et al., 2017) and $17/ MWh for the CHP unit (EPA, 2017). Variable operating costs are esti- mated based on the market price of consumables and waste disposal costs in Turkey (see Table 4).
41: In line with the LCA, the economic assessment takes into account all construction and operating costs, as well as the additional benefits in the supply chain over the lifetime of biogas plants. These benefits in- clude revenues from the heat, electricity and digestate, as well as the premium feed-in-tariff (FIT) for biopower and incentives for the plant equipment (REL, 2010).
To estimate the LCC, discounted cash flow is considered over the life- time of the project as follows:

LCC|life cycle costs of heat and electricity from biogas ($/MWhe+th) LCC1|net life cycle costs of heat and electricity from biogas ($/lifetime) S|net heat and electricity generated in biogas plant (MWhe+th/lifetime) Cc|overnight capital costs ($/lifetime) CO&M,n|non-feed operating and maintenance costs in year n ($/yr) CB,n|cost of biomass production or collection and transport in year n ($/yr) Csv,n|revenues from digestate in year n ($/yr) C1,n|incentives in year n ($/yr) SEn,n|net heat and electricity generated in year n (MWhe+th/yr) i|discount rate (%) t|total lifetime of biogas plant (years).

Furthermore, levelised costs of electricity (LCOE) and levelised costs of heat (LCOH) are calculated considering only installation and operating costs (IEA/NEA, 2015) and costs are allocated between electricity and heat according to their exergy values (for details, see Table S5 in SI).

48: Environmental impacts of energy derived from biogas are com- pared with those from the conventional sources of heat and electric- ity considering the average power-to-heat ratios of CHP systems assumed in this study. For heat generation, natural gas boiler is se- lected as the reference as it is the most common heat source in Turkey (>75%; see Fig. S1 in SI). In the case of electricity, the refer- ence source is Turkish grid (2018), taking into account the latest data on the latter by TEIAS (2019) (see Fig. S1 in the SI). Like the bioenergy systems, the reference systems are modelled in GaBi con- sidering all life cycle stages (fuel acquisition, transport of fuel to the plant, its construction and operation and finally waste disposal) and taking background data from Ecoinvent (2016).

For the economic analysis, the levelised costs of electricity (LCOE) of different power plants in Turkey have been obtained from Atilgan and Azapagic (2016), by adjusting them to the base year considered in this study (2018). This has been carried out by using the exchange rate for ($/TRY) and nominal inflation rate (SU, 2018) for the given period. It is complemented with the recent data in literature (IRENA, 2019). Fur- thermore, the levelised costs of heat (LCOH) of different heat technolo- gies have been sourced from Connoly et al. (2015) and escalated to 2018. As natural gas is the most dominant energy source in the country's heat mix, LCOH of natural gas boiler is computed by using the current costs of installation and operation of the boiler in Turkey (Mimsan, 2015; TUIK, 2019a). Based on these assumptions, levelised costs of grid electricity and heat generation in Turkey are estimated with respect to the corresponding contributions of each source in the energy mix (see Figs. S1 and S2 in SI) and used for the comparison.
49: Environmental impacts of energy derived from biogas are com- pared with those from the conventional sources of heat and electric- ity considering the average power-to-heat ratios of CHP systems assumed in this study. For heat generation, natural gas boiler is se- lected as the reference as it is the most common heat source in Turkey (>75%; see Fig. S1 in SI). In the case of electricity, the refer- ence source is Turkish grid (2018), taking into account the latest data on the latter by TEIAS (2019) (see Fig. S1 in the SI). Like the bioenergy systems, the reference systems are modelled in GaBi con- sidering all life cycle stages (fuel acquisition, transport of fuel to the plant, its construction and operation and finally waste disposal) and taking background data from Ecoinvent (2016).

For the economic analysis, the levelised costs of electricity (LCOE) of different power plants in Turkey have been obtained from Atilgan and Azapagic (2016), by adjusting them to the base year considered in this study (2018). This has been carried out by using the exchange rate for ($/TRY) and nominal inflation rate (SU, 2018) for the given period. It is complemented with the recent data in literature (IRENA, 2019). Fur- thermore, the levelised costs of heat (LCOH) of different heat technolo- gies have been sourced from Connoly et al. (2015) and escalated to 2018. As natural gas is the most dominant energy source in the country's heat mix, LCOH of natural gas boiler is computed by using the current costs of installation and operation of the boiler in Turkey (Mimsan, 2015; TUIK, 2019a). Based on these assumptions, levelised costs of grid electricity and heat generation in Turkey are estimated with respect to the corresponding contributions of each source in the energy mix (see Figs. S1 and S2 in SI) and used for the comparison.
50: A sensitivity analysis has been performed to assess the influence of key parameters and assumptions on the environmental impacts and economic performance of the biogas systems. The effects from the treat- ment of liquid digestate in wastewater treatment plants (WWTP) and

no export of heat are investigated for both LCA and LCC, given a possible lack of the market. To determine the impacts of incentives, the lack of biopower and plant equipment support is also considered, whereas the effect of the availability of heat incentives on LCC is explored in such a case. Finally, discount rates and solid digestate prices are varied to determine their effects on LCC, NPV and PB of the biogas systems. For a summary in the variations in parameters for the economic sensi- tivity analysis, see Table 5.
51: The next two section present and discuss the LCA and LCC results for the biogas systems, respectively, along with a sensitivity analysis and comparison with the reference energy systems and similar studies from literature. The LCA impacts and LCC are then escalated to the na- tional level in Section 3.3 to determine the potential contribution of bio- gas to reducing the GHG emissions and costs of fossil-fuel imports.
52: <header>3.1.1. Environmental impacts of biogas systems </header>

The LCA results in Fig. 2 reveal that all the four systems have net- negative values in at least nine out of 17 categories, indicating net sav- ings in these impacts. The avoided emissions from production and ap- plication of chemical fertilisers are the main reason for the credits in the majority of impacts. Across all the life cycle stages, the burdens from the digestate application are the main hotspot in most of the cate- gories. Overall, Plant B can be considered the best option environmen- tally, with 15 lower impacts out of the 17 considered, while Plant D is the worst alternative, with eight higher impacts than the reference en- ergy mix (see the heatmap in Table 6). These results are discussed in more detail in the following section for each impact in turn, referring to the functional unit of 1 MWh of net electricity and heat generated by each system.

3.1.1.1. Climate change (GWP). The GWP of heat and electricity from the four AD systems ranges from 232 to 892 kg CO2 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The main hotspot for all plants is N20 emitted from the digestate application, accounting for 70-80 of the impact (before credits). On the other hand, the credits from the digestate reduce the overall impact by 43 % to 52 %, depending on its amount in different systems. Due to the lowest total solids ratio and biogas yield from cattle slurry, Plant C produces the highest amount of digestate (see Table 3), thus causing the highest GWP. Conversely, Plant B has the lowest GWP due to the high organics loading rate relative to the other plants and hence has the highest biogas yield. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (in Plant D) has 17 % lower GWP than Plant C which uses only cattle slurry. Fugitive methane emissions from the AD plants ac- count for 8-18% of the total impact.

3.1.1.2. Eutrophication and acidification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP). The release of phosphates from the application of digestate is the major contributor for freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEuP) from the biogas plants using waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C). For Plant D, half the impact is from the cultivation of maize. However,

in all the systems, FWEuP is fully compensated by the credits for the avoided phosphorous emissions from the chemical fertiliser production, resulting in the impact in the range from -11 to - 67 g P eq./MWh, where Plant C is the best and Plant D the worst alternative.
Table 6 Comparison of environmental impacts of 1 MWh of heat and electricity generated by the AD systems in comparison with the reference energy sources.
aFor the impacts nomenclature, see Fig. 2. b1 MWh (net energy output) consists of 0.656 MWh electricity and 0.344 MWh thermal energy as the average of biogas plants (see Table 3).

In the case of MEuP and AP, Plant B has the lowest and Plant C the highest impacts due to the similar reason explained for GWP. The credits for the avoided mineral fertiliser application reduce the two impacts only by up to 35 % and %, respectively. This in turn means that they are net-positive across all the systems, varying between 3 and 16 kg N eq./MWh and 9-31 kg SO2 eq./MWh. The nitrogen- leaching and NH3 emissions from digestate application are major sources of MEuP and AP, respectively. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in 20-25 lower AP and MEuP compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry in Plant C.

<list>3.1.1.3. Ozone depletion potential (ODP). Due to the credits from the re- placed fertilisers, all plants have the net saving for ODP, ranging from 10 to -30 mg CFC-11 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The majority of substances causing ODP (e.g., halons and chlorofluorocar- bons) are released from the fuel used in spreading fertilisers and trans- port of feedstocks, particularly from the refinery and distribution stages (Morales et al., 2015). As the credits associated with the digestate appli- cation are greater than the burdens across the biogas plants, net benefits are the highest for Plant C which produces the largest amount of digestate per MWh and the lowest for Plant B, where most of the digestate is circulated within the digester to maintain the total solids ratio. 
3.1.1.4. Land use and water depletion (LU and WD). Land use and water depletion are both net-negative for the biogas plants that use only waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C) and there is no significant contribu- tion from the operation of the plants. However, Plant D has the net- positive impacts, caused by maize cultivation. Similar to some other im- pacts, the credits, which are proportional to the amount of digestate, are the highest for Plant C and the lowest for Plant B. Hence, Plant C is the best option for these two impacts, and Plant D the worst. 
3.1.1.5. Particulate matter formation (PMF). The direct NH3 and NO emissions from digestate application, which form secondary pollutants in the air, cause the majority of PMF (>849 before credits) and result in the net-positive impact for all the plants. PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.

Due to the credits from the re- placed fertilisers, all plants have the net saving for ODP, ranging from 10 to -30 mg CFC-11 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The majority of substances causing ODP (e.g., halons and chlorofluorocar- bons) are released from the fuel used in spreading fertilisers and trans- port of feedstocks, particularly from the refinery and distribution stages (Morales et al., 2015). As the credits associated with the digestate appli- cation are greater than the burdens across the biogas plants, net benefits are the highest for Plant C which produces the largest amount of digestate per MWh and the lowest for Plant B, where most of the digestate is circulated within the digester to maintain the total solids ratio. 
3.1.1.4. Land use and water depletion (LU and WD). Land use and water depletion are both net-negative for the biogas plants that use only waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C) and there is no significant contribu- tion from the operation of the plants. However, Plant D has the net- positive impacts, caused by maize cultivation. Similar to some other im- pacts, the credits, which are proportional to the amount of digestate, are the highest for Plant C and the lowest for Plant B. Hence, Plant C is the best option for these two impacts, and Plant D the worst. 
3.1.1.5. Particulate matter formation (PMF). The direct NH3 and NO emissions from digestate application, which form secondary pollutants in the air, cause the majority of PMF (>849 before credits) and result in the net-positive impact for all the plants. PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.
55: <header>3.1.1. Environmental impacts of biogas systems </header>

The LCA results in Fig. 2 reveal that all the four systems have net- negative values in at least nine out of 17 categories, indicating net sav- ings in these impacts. The avoided emissions from production and ap- plication of chemical fertilisers are the main reason for the credits in the majority of impacts. Across all the life cycle stages, the burdens from the digestate application are the main hotspot in most of the cate- gories. Overall, Plant B can be considered the best option environmen- tally, with 15 lower impacts out of the 17 considered, while Plant D is the worst alternative, with eight higher impacts than the reference en- ergy mix (see the heatmap in Table 6). These results are discussed in more detail in the following section for each impact in turn, referring to the functional unit of 1 MWh of net electricity and heat generated by each system.

3.1.1.1. Climate change (GWP). The GWP of heat and electricity from the four AD systems ranges from 232 to 892 kg CO2 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The main hotspot for all plants is N20 emitted from the digestate application, accounting for 70-80 of the impact (before credits). On the other hand, the credits from the digestate reduce the overall impact by 43 % to 52 %, depending on its amount in different systems. Due to the lowest total solids ratio and biogas yield from cattle slurry, Plant C produces the highest amount of digestate (see Table 3), thus causing the highest GWP. Conversely, Plant B has the lowest GWP due to the high organics loading rate relative to the other plants and hence has the highest biogas yield. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (in Plant D) has 17 % lower GWP than Plant C which uses only cattle slurry. Fugitive methane emissions from the AD plants ac- count for 8-18% of the total impact.

3.1.1.2. Eutrophication and acidification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP). The release of phosphates from the application of digestate is the major contributor for freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEuP) from the biogas plants using waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C). For Plant D, half the impact is from the cultivation of maize. However,

in all the systems, FWEuP is fully compensated by the credits for the avoided phosphorous emissions from the chemical fertiliser production, resulting in the impact in the range from -11 to - 67 g P eq./MWh, where Plant C is the best and Plant D the worst alternative.

3.1.1.2. Eutrophication and acidification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP). The release of phosphates from the application of digestate is the major contributor for freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEuP) from the biogas plants using waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C). For Plant D, half the impact is from the cultivation of maize. However,

in all the systems, FWEuP is fully compensated by the credits for the avoided phosphorous emissions from the chemical fertiliser production, resulting in the impact in the range from -11 to - 67 g P eq./MWh, where Plant C is the best and Plant D the worst alternative.
Table 6 Comparison of environmental impacts of 1 MWh of heat and electricity generated by the AD systems in comparison with the reference energy sources.
aFor the impacts nomenclature, see Fig. 2. b1 MWh (net energy output) consists of 0.656 MWh electricity and 0.344 MWh thermal energy as the average of biogas plants (see Table 3).

In the case of MEuP and AP, Plant B has the lowest and Plant C the highest impacts due to the similar reason explained for GWP. The credits for the avoided mineral fertiliser application reduce the two impacts only by up to 35 % and %, respectively. This in turn means that they are net-positive across all the systems, varying between 3 and 16 kg N eq./MWh and 9-31 kg SO2 eq./MWh. The nitrogen- leaching and NH3 emissions from digestate application are major sources of MEuP and AP, respectively. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in 20-25 lower AP and MEuP compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry in Plant C.

<list>3.1.1.3. Ozone depletion potential (ODP). Due to the credits from the re- placed fertilisers, all plants have the net saving for ODP, ranging from 10 to -30 mg CFC-11 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The majority of substances causing ODP (e.g., halons and chlorofluorocar- bons) are released from the fuel used in spreading fertilisers and trans- port of feedstocks, particularly from the refinery and distribution stages (Morales et al., 2015). As the credits associated with the digestate appli- cation are greater than the burdens across the biogas plants, net benefits are the highest for Plant C which produces the largest amount of digestate per MWh and the lowest for Plant B, where most of the digestate is circulated within the digester to maintain the total solids ratio. 
3.1.1.4. Land use and water depletion (LU and WD). Land use and water depletion are both net-negative for the biogas plants that use only waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C) and there is no significant contribu- tion from the operation of the plants. However, Plant D has the net- positive impacts, caused by maize cultivation. Similar to some other im- pacts, the credits, which are proportional to the amount of digestate, are the highest for Plant C and the lowest for Plant B. Hence, Plant C is the best option for these two impacts, and Plant D the worst. 
3.1.1.5. Particulate matter formation (PMF). The direct NH3 and NO emissions from digestate application, which form secondary pollutants in the air, cause the majority of PMF (>849 before credits) and result in the net-positive impact for all the plants. PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.
58: <header>3.1.1. Environmental impacts of biogas systems </header>

The LCA results in Fig. 2 reveal that all the four systems have net- negative values in at least nine out of 17 categories, indicating net sav- ings in these impacts. The avoided emissions from production and ap- plication of chemical fertilisers are the main reason for the credits in the majority of impacts. Across all the life cycle stages, the burdens from the digestate application are the main hotspot in most of the cate- gories. Overall, Plant B can be considered the best option environmen- tally, with 15 lower impacts out of the 17 considered, while Plant D is the worst alternative, with eight higher impacts than the reference en- ergy mix (see the heatmap in Table 6). These results are discussed in more detail in the following section for each impact in turn, referring to the functional unit of 1 MWh of net electricity and heat generated by each system.

3.1.1.1. Climate change (GWP). The GWP of heat and electricity from the four AD systems ranges from 232 to 892 kg CO2 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The main hotspot for all plants is N20 emitted from the digestate application, accounting for 70-80 of the impact (before credits). On the other hand, the credits from the digestate reduce the overall impact by 43 % to 52 %, depending on its amount in different systems. Due to the lowest total solids ratio and biogas yield from cattle slurry, Plant C produces the highest amount of digestate (see Table 3), thus causing the highest GWP. Conversely, Plant B has the lowest GWP due to the high organics loading rate relative to the other plants and hence has the highest biogas yield. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (in Plant D) has 17 % lower GWP than Plant C which uses only cattle slurry. Fugitive methane emissions from the AD plants ac- count for 8-18% of the total impact.

PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.
60: <header>3.1.1. Environmental impacts of biogas systems </header>

The LCA results in Fig. 2 reveal that all the four systems have net- negative values in at least nine out of 17 categories, indicating net sav- ings in these impacts. The avoided emissions from production and ap- plication of chemical fertilisers are the main reason for the credits in the majority of impacts. Across all the life cycle stages, the burdens from the digestate application are the main hotspot in most of the cate- gories. Overall, Plant B can be considered the best option environmen- tally, with 15 lower impacts out of the 17 considered, while Plant D is the worst alternative, with eight higher impacts than the reference en- ergy mix (see the heatmap in Table 6). These results are discussed in more detail in the following section for each impact in turn, referring to the functional unit of 1 MWh of net electricity and heat generated by each system.

3.1.1.1. Climate change (GWP). The GWP of heat and electricity from the four AD systems ranges from 232 to 892 kg CO2 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The main hotspot for all plants is N20 emitted from the digestate application, accounting for 70-80 of the impact (before credits). On the other hand, the credits from the digestate reduce the overall impact by 43 % to 52 %, depending on its amount in different systems. Due to the lowest total solids ratio and biogas yield from cattle slurry, Plant C produces the highest amount of digestate (see Table 3), thus causing the highest GWP. Conversely, Plant B has the lowest GWP due to the high organics loading rate relative to the other plants and hence has the highest biogas yield. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (in Plant D) has 17 % lower GWP than Plant C which uses only cattle slurry. Fugitive methane emissions from the AD plants ac- count for 8-18% of the total impact.

3.1.1.2. Eutrophication and acidification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP). The release of phosphates from the application of digestate is the major contributor for freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEuP) from the biogas plants using waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C). For Plant D, half the impact is from the cultivation of maize. However,

in all the systems, FWEuP is fully compensated by the credits for the avoided phosphorous emissions from the chemical fertiliser production, resulting in the impact in the range from -11 to - 67 g P eq./MWh, where Plant C is the best and Plant D the worst alternative.
Table 6 Comparison of environmental impacts of 1 MWh of heat and electricity generated by the AD systems in comparison with the reference energy sources.
aFor the impacts nomenclature, see Fig. 2. b1 MWh (net energy output) consists of 0.656 MWh electricity and 0.344 MWh thermal energy as the average of biogas plants (see Table 3).

In the case of MEuP and AP, Plant B has the lowest and Plant C the highest impacts due to the similar reason explained for GWP. The credits for the avoided mineral fertiliser application reduce the two impacts only by up to 35 % and %, respectively. This in turn means that they are net-positive across all the systems, varying between 3 and 16 kg N eq./MWh and 9-31 kg SO2 eq./MWh. The nitrogen- leaching and NH3 emissions from digestate application are major sources of MEuP and AP, respectively. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in 20-25 lower AP and MEuP compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry in Plant C.

<list>3.1.1.3. Ozone depletion potential (ODP). Due to the credits from the re- placed fertilisers, all plants have the net saving for ODP, ranging from 10 to -30 mg CFC-11 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The majority of substances causing ODP (e.g., halons and chlorofluorocar- bons) are released from the fuel used in spreading fertilisers and trans- port of feedstocks, particularly from the refinery and distribution stages (Morales et al., 2015). As the credits associated with the digestate appli- cation are greater than the burdens across the biogas plants, net benefits are the highest for Plant C which produces the largest amount of digestate per MWh and the lowest for Plant B, where most of the digestate is circulated within the digester to maintain the total solids ratio.

Land use and water depletion are both net-negative for the biogas plants that use only waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C) and there is no significant contribu- tion from the operation of the plants. However, Plant D has the net- positive impacts, caused by maize cultivation. Similar to some other im- pacts, the credits, which are proportional to the amount of digestate, are the highest for Plant C and the lowest for Plant B. Hence, Plant C is the best option for these two impacts, and Plant D the worst.

3.1.1.2. Eutrophication and acidification potentials (FWEuP, MEuP and AP). The release of phosphates from the application of digestate is the major contributor for freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEuP) from the biogas plants using waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C). For Plant D, half the impact is from the cultivation of maize. However,

in all the systems, FWEuP is fully compensated by the credits for the avoided phosphorous emissions from the chemical fertiliser production, resulting in the impact in the range from -11 to - 67 g P eq./MWh, where Plant C is the best and Plant D the worst alternative.
Table 6 Comparison of environmental impacts of 1 MWh of heat and electricity generated by the AD systems in comparison with the reference energy sources.
aFor the impacts nomenclature, see Fig. 2. b1 MWh (net energy output) consists of 0.656 MWh electricity and 0.344 MWh thermal energy as the average of biogas plants (see Table 3).

In the case of MEuP and AP, Plant B has the lowest and Plant C the highest impacts due to the similar reason explained for GWP. The credits for the avoided mineral fertiliser application reduce the two impacts only by up to 35 % and %, respectively. This in turn means that they are net-positive across all the systems, varying between 3 and 16 kg N eq./MWh and 9-31 kg SO2 eq./MWh. The nitrogen- leaching and NH3 emissions from digestate application are major sources of MEuP and AP, respectively. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in 20-25 lower AP and MEuP compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry in Plant C.

<list>3.1.1.3. Ozone depletion potential (ODP). Due to the credits from the re- placed fertilisers, all plants have the net saving for ODP, ranging from 10 to -30 mg CFC-11 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The majority of substances causing ODP (e.g., halons and chlorofluorocar- bons) are released from the fuel used in spreading fertilisers and trans- port of feedstocks, particularly from the refinery and distribution stages (Morales et al., 2015). As the credits associated with the digestate appli- cation are greater than the burdens across the biogas plants, net benefits are the highest for Plant C which produces the largest amount of digestate per MWh and the lowest for Plant B, where most of the digestate is circulated within the digester to maintain the total solids ratio. 
3.1.1.4. Land use and water depletion (LU and WD). Land use and water depletion are both net-negative for the biogas plants that use only waste feedstocks (Plants A, B and C) and there is no significant contribu- tion from the operation of the plants. However, Plant D has the net- positive impacts, caused by maize cultivation. Similar to some other im- pacts, the credits, which are proportional to the amount of digestate, are the highest for Plant C and the lowest for Plant B. Hence, Plant C is the best option for these two impacts, and Plant D the worst. 
3.1.1.5. Particulate matter formation (PMF). The direct NH3 and NO emissions from digestate application, which form secondary pollutants in the air, cause the majority of PMF (>849 before credits) and result in the net-positive impact for all the plants. PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.
63: <header>3.1.1. Environmental impacts of biogas systems </header>

The LCA results in Fig. 2 reveal that all the four systems have net- negative values in at least nine out of 17 categories, indicating net sav- ings in these impacts. The avoided emissions from production and ap- plication of chemical fertilisers are the main reason for the credits in the majority of impacts. Across all the life cycle stages, the burdens from the digestate application are the main hotspot in most of the cate- gories. Overall, Plant B can be considered the best option environmen- tally, with 15 lower impacts out of the 17 considered, while Plant D is the worst alternative, with eight higher impacts than the reference en- ergy mix (see the heatmap in Table 6). These results are discussed in more detail in the following section for each impact in turn, referring to the functional unit of 1 MWh of net electricity and heat generated by each system.

3.1.1.1. Climate change (GWP). The GWP of heat and electricity from the four AD systems ranges from 232 to 892 kg CO2 eq./MWh for Plants B and C, respectively. The main hotspot for all plants is N20 emitted from the digestate application, accounting for 70-80 of the impact (before credits). On the other hand, the credits from the digestate reduce the overall impact by 43 % to 52 %, depending on its amount in different systems. Due to the lowest total solids ratio and biogas yield from cattle slurry, Plant C produces the highest amount of digestate (see Table 3), thus causing the highest GWP. Conversely, Plant B has the lowest GWP due to the high organics loading rate relative to the other plants and hence has the highest biogas yield. Moreover, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (in Plant D) has 17 % lower GWP than Plant C which uses only cattle slurry. Fugitive methane emissions from the AD plants ac- count for 8-18% of the total impact.

PMF is the highest for Plant C (3.5 PM2.5 eq./MWh) and the lowest for Plant B PM2.5 eq./MWh) in line with the amount of digestate produced by these plants. Similar to AP, the co-digestion of cattle slurry with maize silage (Plant D) results in lower PMF impact compared to Plant C which uses cattle slurry as a feedstock. 
3.1.1.6. Photochemical oxidants formation (POF) - human and ecosystems. The POF-human and ecosystems potentials are estimated at 0.77-1.57 NO eq./MWh. This impact is mainly (35-40%) associated with NOx and NMVOC emissions released from the biogas combustion, followed by digestate application (30-40%). The credits for replacement of min- eral fertiliser reduce POF by 50% Plant B with the highest biogas yield has the lowest impact, while Plant D using silage has the highest impact due to the concurrent effects of digestate application and crop cultiva- tion. 
3.1.1.7. Fossil depletion and mineral depletion (FD and MD). Due to the credits for the avoided fertilisers, FD and MD are net-negative for all the biogas plants, ranging from -24 to -81 kg oil eq./MWh for FD and - 0.6 to 1.5 kg Cu eq./MWh for MD. Due to the credits for the avoidance of chemical fertilisers, the highest savings in the resources are achieved in Plant C and the lowest in Plant B. Despite the improved biogas potential, adding maize silage to cattle slurry (in Plant D) reduces these benefits by around 50% compared to Plant C due to the additional impacts from maize cultivation. The consumption of diesel for feedstock transport and farming activities is the main contributor to FD, while the construction of agricultural machinery and trucks are mainly account- able for MD.</list>

3.1.1.8. Human health (HT-c, HT-nc) and ecotoxicity (FET, MET and TET). The toxicity impacts for all the plants are net-negative (except for HT- C from Plant D) due to the credits for avoided fertilisers. In the case of Plant D, due to the additional burdens from silage production, the total burdens for HT-c are higher than the credits. However, Plant D has the highest net savings for HT-nc owing to the heavy metal uptake by silage crops. The Plant C has the highest net savings for FET and MET and Plant D for TET. Again, this is due to the higher digestate production in Plant C, hence higher credits for the avoided fertilisers. However, for TET, the effect of credits is reduced due to the additional emissions from the diesel use in feedstock transport and in digestate application.
65: Table 6 compares LCA results for the AD plants with the reference energy sources. Plants A and B outperform the reference sources in 15 of 17 environmental categories (except only MEuP and AP), while Plants C and D have lower impacts in nine categories. All AD plants pro- vide 100 savings in eight categories (FWEuP, ODP, FD, MD, HT-nc, FET, MET and TET) and > 95 in HT-c. In the case of GWP, only co- digestion of organic wastes and cattle slurry (Plant B) outperforms the conventional energy mix with a 56 % lower impact, while the co- digestion of chicken and cattle solid manure (Plant A) has a similar im- pact to the conventional energy mix. However, the bioenergy from cat- tle slurry (in Plants C and D) has 40-70% higher GWP and 10-70 higher POFs and PMF than the grid electricity and heat from natural gas. Moreover, the bioenergy from waste feedstocks has >4 times higher AP and MEuP than the fossil-fuel derived energy.
66: As mentioned earlier, two parameters are considered here: heat re- covery and liquid digestate management.

3.1.3.1. Heat recovery. In line with the data provided by plant owners in Turkey (DiMuh, 2017; ENCEV, 2016), surplus heat from the AD plants is assumed to be exported to nearby sites in the base case. However, in practice, this depends on the heat demand of the selected region and, in most cases, it is likely that no heat would be exported from small- scale AD plants, such as those used on farms (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2017). Therefore, this is assumed in the sensitivity analysis. As no heat is exported, and hence the heat is no longer a co-product, the functional unit in that case is '1 MWh of net electricity generated in a biogas plant'. The results (in Fig. S3 in SI) suggest that the power gen- eration without exporting any heat leads to an 18 % increase in GWP, MEuP, AP, PMF, POFs across the plants; in addition, LU and WD for Plant D also go up. On the contrary, the impacts that were net- negative in the base case (i.e., FWEuP, ODP, FD, MD and all the toxicities) are reduced by 18% as the digestate credits are no longer split between the heat and electricity but awarded to electricity generation alone. This outcome should be carefully evaluated as it might change depending on the method chosen for the attribution of emissions to electricity in the base case. Compared to grid power, the ranking of biogas plants remains the same as discussed in Section 3.1.2. The exceptions to this are POFs (for Plant A) and PMF (Plant D) which become comparable to the im- pacts of grid electricity as opposed to their lower impacts in the base case.

3.1.3.2. Liquid digestate management. In the base case, it is assumed that liquid digestate is applied to agricultural fields along with solid digestate. However, considering that the liquid fraction of digestate has low level of nutrients, this may not be economically feasible for small-scale AD plants because of the high cost of transport and applica- tion (Lukehurst et al., 2010). Furthermore, the spreading of digestate on lands is restricted in designated "nitrogen vulnerable zones" in Turkey to 170 kg N/ha (DiMuh, 2017). Therefore, in this sensitivity analysis, it is considered that the raw digestate is separated into the solid and liquid fractions as in the base case, but the liquid digestate is treated in a

WWTP instead of spreading it onto land, whilst the solid fraction is still applied on farmland as in the base case. The LCA data for the WWTP have been sourced from Ecoinvent (2016), assuming similar properties (e.g., BOD, COD) with the wastewater from AD of whey. Overall, due to the lack of credits for organic fertilisers for liquid digestate, most of the impacts would increase slightly (<10%), except for FWEuP, WD, AP, PMF and TET (see Fig. S4 in SI). Moreover, Plant B shows a higher sensi- tivity to this parameter due to the greater loss of nutrients retained in liquid digestate from this plant in comparison to the other plants (see Table S2). For all the biogas plants, FWEuP would be much higher (>5 times) compared to the base case, which may be explained by the differ- ences in the modelling assumptions for phosphate leaching from WWTP and digestate application in the base case. Similarly, the clean water discharge from WWTP would reduce WD by 17-58% in all plants. With the application of only solid digestate on farmland in this case, AP and PMF would also be lower compared to the base case (spreading of both fractions of digestate): by up to 25 % for Plant B but <6% for the other plants. Nevertheless, in comparison to the reference energy sources, all biogas plants still have the same ranking as in the base case (Section 3.1.2), except for Plant C for FWEuP, which is now up to 100 % higher than for the reference energy mix.

70: As mentioned in the introduction, a number of LCA studies have assessed environmental impacts of bioenergy derived from biogas. However, due to the differences in operating parameters (e.g., type of feedstock) and methodological aspects (e.g., system boundaries and as- sumptions), there is a large variability in results. Therefore, comparison is possible only for GWP. Moreover, majority of studies found in the lit- erature conducted an evaluation choosing electricity generation as the main function of biogas systems. Thus, to facilitate the comparison, the impacts from the current study (per MWhe+th. have been recalculated by allocating them to electricity generation (per MWhe) with or without crediting the system for the avoidance of heat from natural gas, depending on the approach in the study used in comparison.

In general, the GWP of Plant B (208 kg CO2 eq./MWhe with credits for heat) is within the range of -1720 to 550 kg CO2 eq./MWh. reported in the review by Bacenetti et al. (2016). The results for the other biogas plants in this study (644-1180 kg CO2 eq./MWhe with heat credits) are up to twice as high as the maximum value reported in that review. This is because the manure digestion in the reviewed studies was credited for the avoided impacts from traditional manure management which was not the case in the current work. For a similar reason, the GWP of Plant A in the current study (688 kg CO2 eq./MWh. without credits for heat) differs considerably from that in Fusi et al. (2016) (-395 kg CO2 eq./MWhe). For a similar kind of biogas plant (co-digestion of maize silage and manure), Fusi et al. (2016) and Lijó et al. (2017) found also a considerably lower impact (291-408 kg CO2 eq./MWhe) than here (930 kg CO2 eq./MWhe without heat credits) which can be attributed primarily to the assumptions for some stages (e.g., emission factors from digestate appli- cation), beside differences in the displaced systems. On the other hand, in comparison of co-digestion of animal manure with different wastes, the findings of this study (232 kg CO2 eq./MWhe and 306 kg CO2 eq./MWh. without credits for heat) compare well with that of the similar biogas plants in Whiting and Azapagic (2014) (266 kg CO2 eq./ MWhe+th) but are lower than those in Bacenetti et al. (2018) (380 kg eq./MWhe) due to the consideration of closed storage of digestate here.

72: In this section, the life cycle costs are discussed first, followed by levelised costs of energy, net present value, and payback time.
Fig. 3. Life cycle costs of 1 MWh of electricity and heat generated in biogas plants utilising different feedstocks.
[*Incentives refer to the electricity sales at the premium rate for bioelectricity (i.e. feed-in- tariff) and locally-produced equipment].
73: As shown in Fig. 3, the life cycle costs of bioenergy from the AD sys- items range between -$6.1 and -$15.3 per MWh indicating net profits per MWh, generation in biogas plants. The feed-in-tariff (FIT) incentives play a major role in compensating the operating and in- stallation costs of all biogas plants over their lifetime, accounting be- tween 50 and 77 % of all benefits from the energy conversion process. Digestate revenues constitute also a significant (40-50%) part in the plants using cattle slurry (Plants C and D). The latter also have higher feedstock production and collection costs than Plants A and B. This is mainly due to the lower biogas yield per amount of feedstock in Plant C and costs incurred by maize silage cultivation in Plant D. Capital and operating costs per unit of energy are roughly similar for all the plants.
74: <header>3.2.2. Levelised costs of energy, net present value and payback time </header>

As indicated in Table 7, all biogas plants have net profits (NPV > 0) and pay back in around two years. Adding maize silage to Plant D re- sults in 24 % higher NPV than for Plant C, while co-digestion of slurry with organic wastes (Plant B) generates 17 % higher profits in compar- ison to Plant A. However, the payback times would increase in such a case despite being shorter than a year. More than 60 % of revenue is from electricity, with heat contributing <10%. This is due to both the lower price of heat (Table 4) and the relatively small amount of heat exported (0.46-0.56 MWhth/MWhe).

Levelised costs of electricity and heat generation are presented in Fig. 4. The lowest LCOE are for Plant A, which are estimated at $61/ MWhe, followed closely by Plant B ($63/MWhe), while the highest are for Plant D ($93/MWh)). The LCOE for Plants A and B are 26 % lower than for grid power ($84/MWhe; for the latter, see Fig. S2 in SI), while they are comparable in case of Plant C and 10% higher for Plant D.

In terms of LCOH, all biogas plants have significantly lower costs (75-83%) compared to the heat mix ($67/MWhth), as well as the heat from natural gas (see Fig. S2). Among the different feedstocks, Plants A and B have around 35 % lower LCOH than the other plants owing to the free source of feedstocks (i.e., wastes) and the high biogas yield.

76: The effects of different parameters on LCC, NPV and PB of the biogas systems are investigated in this part of study. The variations in the
77: Net present values and payback periods of energy from different feedstocks through bio- gas.
|Net present value ($ million/lifetime)|Payback time (years) Plant A|4.7|1.8 Plant B|5.5|2.0 Plant C|5.0|1.6 Plant D|6.2|2.1
Fig. 4. Levelised costs of (a) electricity and (b) heat generation in biogas plants using different feedstocks.

results relative to the base case are presented in Fig. 5 for Plant A (for the other plants, see Fig. S5 in SI). As can be seen in Fig. 5, the LCC and NPV are very sensitive to the solid digestate price which can vary between $7-$52/t. Furthermore, all the investment costs in Plants C and D would pay back in the first year given the higher amount of digestate from these plants.

Biopower incentives and the utilisation of liquid digestate are other key parameters with significant influence on the economic performance of biogas plants. With the lack of biopower incentives and liquid digestate market, LCC and PB time would increase while NPV would de- crease. Without the incentives, the LCC for all the biogas plants would increase up to three times with values above zero per unit of energy as opposed to being net-negative in the base case. NPV would also de- crease by around 54-77 % while PB would increase by 3.3 to 6.5 years for all the plants.

The LCC would increase by 50-130% if liquid digestate is treated in WWTP due to the reduction in revenues from this by-product. Never- theless, LCC would still be net-negative for Plants A and C although the PB time for Plant C would increase from 2 to 3.4 years due to the higher amount of liquid digestate and the cost associated with its treat- ment.
If heat incentives are available at the current heat market price, bio- gas systems would generate >50 % higher profits per MWhe+ On the

other hand, since a significant portion of heat is already utilised in the process itself, the availability of heat market has a very small effect on the PB time and NPV compared to the other parameters. However, it should be noted that LCOE would increase by around 10 % ($68-$101/ MWhe) if heat is wasted, but Plants A and B would continue to be cost-competitive against the grid power. Finally, these results suggest that LCC of biogas plants would remain lower than zero for the variation in majority of the parameters and all plants would be feasible (NPV > 0) even under the worst-case scenarios. The PB time is also largely un- affected with less than one-year change in most cases, except for biopower incentives.
3.2.4. Comparison of levelised costs with literature

The LCOE of biogas plants calculated here ($61-$93/MWh.) fall within the range of costs ($25-$280/MWh.) reported in IEA/NEA (2015) for European biogas plants. Similar ranges are also reported in Gerssen-Gondelach et al. (2014) ($49-$107/MWh.) and IRENA (2012) ($40-$88/MWh.), regardless of feedstocks, matching well the results in this study. Compared to the global average cost of electricity from biomass ($62/MWh.; IRENA, 2019), the LCOE of Plants A and B are 10-15 % lower. Moreover, the payback time is found lower in this study compared to others (3.5-5 years) in Turkey (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Balaman and Selim, 2014) because of the higher plant size and

Fig. 5. Effect of different economic parameters on LCC, NPV and payback time for Plant A. [LCC: life cycle costs, NPV: net present value, PB: payback time. All values are expressed relative to the base case. The lighter shade represents the desired change and the darker the op- posite].

capacity assumed here. Furthermore, the cost of electricity from a biogas plant utilising manure in Italy ($124/MWhe; Venanzi et al., 2018) is substantially higher than LCOE estimated in this study, which may be due to the differences in operating costs of biogas plants in different countries.
78: Net present values and payback periods of energy from different feedstocks through bio- gas.
|Net present value ($ million/lifetime)|Payback time (years) Plant A|4.7|1.8 Plant B|5.5|2.0 Plant C|5.0|1.6 Plant D|6.2|2.1
Fig. 4. Levelised costs of (a) electricity and (b) heat generation in biogas plants using different feedstocks.

results relative to the base case are presented in Fig. 5 for Plant A (for the other plants, see Fig. S5 in SI). As can be seen in Fig. 5, the LCC and NPV are very sensitive to the solid digestate price which can vary between $7-$52/t. Furthermore, all the investment costs in Plants C and D would pay back in the first year given the higher amount of digestate from these plants.

Biopower incentives and the utilisation of liquid digestate are other key parameters with significant influence on the economic performance of biogas plants. With the lack of biopower incentives and liquid digestate market, LCC and PB time would increase while NPV would de- crease. Without the incentives, the LCC for all the biogas plants would increase up to three times with values above zero per unit of energy as opposed to being net-negative in the base case. NPV would also de- crease by around 54-77 % while PB would increase by 3.3 to 6.5 years for all the plants.

The LCC would increase by 50-130% if liquid digestate is treated in WWTP due to the reduction in revenues from this by-product. Never- theless, LCC would still be net-negative for Plants A and C although the PB time for Plant C would increase from 2 to 3.4 years due to the higher amount of liquid digestate and the cost associated with its treat- ment.
If heat incentives are available at the current heat market price, bio- gas systems would generate >50 % higher profits per MWhe+ On the

other hand, since a significant portion of heat is already utilised in the process itself, the availability of heat market has a very small effect on the PB time and NPV compared to the other parameters. However, it should be noted that LCOE would increase by around 10 % ($68-$101/ MWhe) if heat is wasted, but Plants A and B would continue to be cost-competitive against the grid power. Finally, these results suggest that LCC of biogas plants would remain lower than zero for the variation in majority of the parameters and all plants would be feasible (NPV > 0) even under the worst-case scenarios. The PB time is also largely un- affected with less than one-year change in most cases, except for biopower incentives.
3.2.4. Comparison of levelised costs with literature

The LCOE of biogas plants calculated here ($61-$93/MWh.) fall within the range of costs ($25-$280/MWh.) reported in IEA/NEA (2015) for European biogas plants. Similar ranges are also reported in Gerssen-Gondelach et al. (2014) ($49-$107/MWh.) and IRENA (2012) ($40-$88/MWh.), regardless of feedstocks, matching well the results in this study. Compared to the global average cost of electricity from biomass ($62/MWh.; IRENA, 2019), the LCOE of Plants A and B are 10-15 % lower. Moreover, the payback time is found lower in this study compared to others (3.5-5 years) in Turkey (Aksay and Tabak, 2022; Balaman and Selim, 2014) because of the higher plant size and

82: <header>3.3. Environmental and economic impacts at the national level </header>

This section considers potential environmental and economic impli- cations for Turkey of bioenergy from AD systems. For the former, the focus is on GHG emission as the main policy driver and for the latter, the emphasis is on cost savings relative to fossil-fuel alternatives.

In Turkey, the poultry sector is well-organized with high availability and accessibility of chicken manure, while the dairy farms are mostly traditional, small-scale and use free-grazing method (DBFZ, 2011; FAO, 2016). Therefore, 50 of dairy manure and 100% of chicken poul- try are assumed to be available for bioenergy production (Ersoy and Ugurlu, 2020). Following this, the potential of cattle and chicken ma- nure is estimated at 97.3 Mt/y and 14.4 Mt/y, respectively, which are calculated based on the number of animals (including layer and broiler chickens, and beef and dairy cattle; TUIK, 2019b) and daily manure pro- duction per head of animal (Ersoy and Ugurlu, 2020). The amount of
vegetable and fruit residues is estimated at 15-20 Mt/y, accounting for 25 % of total agricultural residues and food losses in retail (GDRE, 2018; Salihoglu et al., 2018).

If all available chicken manure (limiting factor) in Turkey is utilised for energy through co-digestion with other wastes, 30 % and 60 of all available cattle manure would be utilised in Plants A and B, respec- tively. As can be seen in Fig. 6a and b, despite insignificant savings via Plant A (<0.1 %), Plant B could save 12.4 Mt CO2 eq., or 2.5 % of national emissions, utilising 58.4 Mt of cattle manure (see Table 6). However, this would require collection of other organic wastes (e.g., household) in addition to that available from the retail sector (15-20 Mt/y). Regarding the digestate use on land (Fig. 6c), this also has a potential to displace 37 % of nitrogen, 25 % of phosphate and > 100 % of potassium fertilisers consumed in Turkey (see Section 2.2.3). However, if Plants C and D are used to convert all available cattle slurry to bioenergy, national GHG emissions would increase by 1 % due to a higher GWP from digestate application (see Table 6). Further, 3.5 % of total arable land (or 670,000 ha; TUIK, 2018b) would need to be allo- cated for maize silage production (Plant D) in such a case considering 50 t/ha yield (see Section S1.1 in SI).
In terms of economic implications (see Fig. 6a and d), the bioenergy from Plant B could potentially meet 3 % (3.6 mtoe/y) of total energy
Plant A Plant B Plant C Plant D
Plant A Plant B Plant C Plant D

Fig. 6. Potential contributions to reduction in energy consumption, GHG emissions and costs (a), GHG reduction per unit energy conversion (b), mineral fertiliser production (c), and ef- fects at the national level for different biogas plants (c&d). [*Imported fuels refer to natural gas and coal. In 2018, the energy consumption was 103 mtoe (IEA, 2019) and GHG emissions were 526 Mt CO2 eq. (TUIK, 2019c). Savings are calculated based on the fuel price ($105/t for coal, $0.175/Nm³ natural gas) and plant efficiency (36 % for coal power plants; 98 % for natural gas boiler) (Atilgan and Azapagic, 2016; EMRA, 2018b; TKI, 2019)].

consumption and save 10% of total fuel imports, equivalent to bn/y, considering the replacement of electricity from coal and heat from nat- ural gas. These are the lowest for Plants A and C which are half of the savings from Plant D with 0.8 mtoe/y of energy potential and $0.3 bn/ y (or of cost savings (see Fig. 6a and b).

Following this, the potential of cattle and chicken ma- nure is estimated at 97.3 Mt/y and 14.4 Mt/y, respectively, which are calculated based on the number of animals (including layer and broiler chickens, and beef and dairy cattle; TUIK, 2019b) and daily manure pro- duction per head of animal (Ersoy and Ugurlu, 2020). The amount of
vegetable and fruit residues is estimated at 15-20 Mt/y, accounting for 25 % of total agricultural residues and food losses in retail (GDRE, 2018; Salihoglu et al., 2018).

If all available chicken manure (limiting factor) in Turkey is utilised for energy through co-digestion with other wastes, 30 % and 60 of all available cattle manure would be utilised in Plants A and B, respec- tively. As can be seen in Fig. 6a and b, despite insignificant savings via Plant A (<0.1 %), Plant B could save 12.4 Mt CO2 eq., or 2.5 % of national emissions, utilising 58.4 Mt of cattle manure (see Table 6). However, this would require collection of other organic wastes (e.g., household) in addition to that available from the retail sector (15-20 Mt/y). Regarding the digestate use on land (Fig. 6c), this also has a potential to displace 37 % of nitrogen, 25 % of phosphate and > 100 % of potassium fertilisers consumed in Turkey (see Section 2.2.3). However, if Plants C and D are used to convert all available cattle slurry to bioenergy, national GHG emissions would increase by 1 % due to a higher GWP from digestate application (see Table 6). Further, 3.5 % of total arable land (or 670,000 ha; TUIK, 2018b) would need to be allo- cated for maize silage production (Plant D) in such a case considering 50 t/ha yield (see Section S1.1 in SI).
In terms of economic implications (see Fig. 6a and d), the bioenergy from Plant B could potentially meet 3 % (3.6 mtoe/y) of total energy
