87: One of the limitations of this study is related to the data which are specific to the proposed plants in Turkey. These may not correspond to the conditions once these plants are operational and a small variation in these, such as feedstock properties and the plant scale, may have a considerable impact on the results, particularly in relation to the biogas yield. Therefore, further studies are necessary to analyse biogas systems for different feedstocks and their mixtures and to determine the optimal conditions that maximise biogas production.

As mentioned in Section 3.1.4, the selection of system boundaries has a great effect on LCA results. Therefore, the environmental perfor- mance of biogas plants relative to the current energy sources needs to be interpreted with caution, particularly for Plants C and D. Unlike farm-scale biogas plants, where the avoided emissions from the storage and disposal of animal manure are considered, this study evaluated centralised agricultural biogas plants fed primarily with farm waste and collected from nearby farms. In this regard, changing the system boundary to include avoided impacts from alternative management and disposal of wastes could reverse the results of this study leading to the lowest impacts for Plants C and D instead of Plants A and B (see Table 6). There has been no consensus on how to account for animal manure in LCA studies (i.e. as by-product or waste; Esteves et al., 2019), so future research evaluating this critical point is needed, as well as to find out if the biogas-efficiency increase due to adding co- substrates offsets the impacts from their production or transport. Fur- thermore, as there is no cultivation of energy crops in Turkey, research is also needed to analyse their effects on AD of animal manure and to de- fine the most promising crops, as well as their optimal ratio in the mix.

Another limitation of this study regarding the use of maize silage as a co-substrate is that it only accounts for direct impacts from cultivation. However, expanding its cultivation and use at a larger ratio (beyond 25 % considered here) in the mix may trigger competition for land used for food and feed (i.e. direct and indirect land use change impacts; Beausang et al., 2021), which need to be addressed in the future along with land-rental costs in the economic analysis. Moreover, the dataset considered for maize silage in this study was based on an average crop yield in Turkey. However, this is also restricted with agricultural param- eters (e.g. climate and precipitation) and can change temporally along with the levels of irrigation and fertilisation required, which subse- quently can affect environmental impacts and agricultural production costs.

Certain limitations pertain to the nutrient value of digestate, which was estimated according to the literature considering the type of input biomass, separation technique used and the plant availability factor of each nutrient within the digestate. Moreover, the benefits of digestate are highly dependent on the type of crop production and fertilisers re- placed (Zhang et al., 2021), instead of the Turkish fertiliser mix assumed here. Detailed data on the digestate properties and the impacts from its use on croplands are relatively scarce in literature and should be inves- tigated through field trials. This is also valid for emission factors from the application of digestate and chemical fertilisers, which can differ considerably between regions due to differing climatic conditions and soil properties, as well as the methods applied.

The energy systems displaced, the method for the calculation of levelised costs and the variability of incentives and economic data over time present further limitations in this study. The LCA results were benchmarked against Turkish grid mix and natural gas heat; how- ever, the relative performance of biogas plants could be different de- pending on the systems substituted elsewhere and considered in the

comparison. Moreover, there is no consensus on how to allocate im- pacts or costs between heat and electricity supplied by CHP plants. This may lead to different results if an allocation method other than exergy used here is applied. Finally, the economic analysis in this study was based on the year 2018 after which some economic parame- ters (e.g. the inflation rate) have changed considerably. Furthermore, in 2021, an amendment on the renewable energy law was announced (Of- ficial Gazette, 2021), introducing a new price for biopower incentives in Turkish lira rather than US$, but defining an upper limit in the latter ($86/MWh.). Although a wide range of possible situations was included in our analysis, further investigation on the feasibility of biogas plants is required to take into account these recent changes and to define additional incentives required to make the operation of biogas plants economically viable, including externalised benefits (e.g. carbon).

As this study intended to provide general insights on the biogas sus- tainability in Turkey, the compounded effect of relevant limitations should be investigated further when these plants are established in cer- tain regions. Further studies filling the temporal and geographical gaps in life cycle inventory data would also support the future development of LCA studies, allowing a better understanding of the effect of data and methodological choices on the uncertainties in results. Lastly, future research can be expanded to assess the social aspects of energy produc- tion through biogas to provide a more comprehensive picture of sus- tainability of these systems.

93: This study has evaluated the life cycle environmental and economic implications of electricity and heat generation from agricultural resides through anaerobic digestion in Turkey. The results show that, regardless of the feedstock, energy from biogas provides net environmental sav- ings in eight out of 17 categories and net-negative LCC (from -$6.1 to -$15.3 MWhe+th). Following this, all biogas systems are found profitable and pay back well within their lifetime (~2 years). This is mainly due to the revenues from using both fractions of digestate as fertilisers and the premium FIT incentives for biopower in Turkey. Digestate application is also the major contributor to most environmen- tal impacts, while operational costs, including biomass supply, play a key role for the overall LCC, especially when cattle slurry, which has a low biogas yield, is used as a feedstock.

Compared to heat from natural gas and grid electricity, energy from biogas plants has lower impacts in at least nine environmental catego- ries. However, using feedstocks with the high solid content and biogas yield (Plants A and B) is environmentally better in 15 categories, includ- ing GWP. On the other hand, the utilisation of cattle slurry (with or without maize silage in Plants C and D) results in 40-70 % higher impacts than the reference sources of energy in GWP, POF and PMF, in addition to large increases in acidification and marine eutrophication (>4 times). Compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry (Plant adding maize silage to the process (Plant could improve the environ- mental sustainability of energy conversion from cattle slurry for those categories where the digestate application matters (i.e., GWP, AP, MEuP, PMF). However, this leads to trade-offs in other environmental categories and costs (LCC and levelised costs) because the cultivation of maize silage implies higher costs (relative to free waste feedstocks) and impacts in certain categories (e.g., land use and water depletion), as well as lower credits associated with the replacement of chemical fertilisers.

In terms of levelised costs, all biogas plants outperform heat from natural gas (75-83 % while only Plants A and B perform better than the grid electricity (26%) and Plant C generates electricity at a cost comparable to it. Furthermore, Plants A and B continue to be cost- competitive against grid power even if the heat is not exported, although this would result in about 20 % higher impacts in some categories, including GWP. To put these results in context, it is

estimated that, if all available chicken manure and 60% of cattle manure in Turkey are co-digested with other wastes, it could potentially save 12.2 Mt CO2 eq. (or 2.5 %) of annual GHG emissions and $1.3 bn (or 10%) of fossil fuel imports, as well as 37% of nitrogen, 25% of phosphate and > 100 % of potassium fertilisers consumed annually. This study indicates that biogas systems using primarily waste feed- stocks have a potential to increase the environmental and economic sustainability of energy in Turkey. Although the robustness of results has been tested considering a wide range of data and alternative scenar- ios in the sensitivity analysis, it should be noted that all these findings are dependent on the assumptions considered in the study, as well as the data used in the modelling.

To reduce the emissions from digestate application, alternative digestate treatment and application technologies should be consid- ered, such as composting, drying or evaporation of digestate. Based upon the differences in results for biogas plants in this study, feed- stock properties (e.g., biogas yield and total solids) seem to be the most crucial parameter in the sustainability of biogas plants, affect- ing each unit operation in the system. Thus, a further study should evaluate the improvement opportunities of the sustainability in bio- gas systems using cattle slurry and focus on feedstock composition (e.g., cattle slurry with different mixing ratios of alternative feed- stocks). The pre-seperation of cattle slurry can also reduce the asso- ciated impacts from the application of very dilute digestate and increase the biogas yield. Moreover, any efforts to develop sustain- able practices in maize silage cultivation (e.g., renewable power based-irrigation, organic fertilisation) could improve the sustain- ability of those plants using it.

In summary, from an environmental and economic perspective, an- aerobic digestion of agricultural wastes could help to reduce GHG emis- sions and fossil fuel import costs in Turkey, providing cleaner, more secure and affordable energy, as well as organic fertilisers. The policy recommendations to accelerate this transition are outlined below:

<list>Anaerobic digestion of wet agricultural wastes combined with the cir- cular agricultural bioeconomy should be encouraged by providing ad- ditional incentives for renewable heat and organic fertilisers. Balancing these with the existing biopower incentives would help to promote decarbonisation in a range of sectors, such as heat and agri- culture, in addition to the electricity sector. 
Organic wastes with high solid content could be utilised effectively to support the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants in regions where animal manure has a large potential; there- fore, the collection effort for these wastes should be increased by local authorities. 
In order to achieve maximum benefits from biogas plants, it is impor- tant to ensure the availability of the market for heat and digestate. Therefore, the region- site-specific data regarding the annual heat demand and the possibility of digestate application to nearby fields should be considered prior to the deployment of biogas plants. 
In this regard, there may be a further need for mapping heat demand across the country and developing infrastructure for district heating systems. The alternative ways of resource recovery from digestate and the effective utilisation of digestate in the crop cultivation should also be investigated and mapped across different regions of Turkey. 
Given the growing global biogas market, maize silage should also be included in Turkey's energy portfolio for its co-digestion with cattle slurry at an optimum ratio to increase the biogas yield without compromising the cost and environmental benefits. However, a fur- ther study analysing the consequential impacts of switching lands from feed to energy crop is also necessary to understand its full life cycle impacts before its wide-scale cultivation. 
It is necessary to develop a policy framework for sustainable biogas production from biomass resources and its optimal use in different end-use sectors engaging with a broad community of different stake- holders and assessing the life cycle sustainability of short-term and</list>
long-term scenarios at local, regional and national levels.

An integrated policy approach covering different sectors (i.e. energy, transport, waste and agriculture) is essential to realise all synergies and benefits associated with biogas systems. Although the scope of this analysis was limited to the biogas utilisation for heat and electric- ity generation via its combustion in a CHP plant, there might be other favourable pathways (e.g. upgrading to biomethane as an equivalent fuel to natural gas to be used in both households and vehicles) to achieve national goals pertaining to energy, economy, environment and agriculture.

On the other hand, the utilisation of cattle slurry (with or without maize silage in Plants C and D) results in 40-70 % higher impacts than the reference sources of energy in GWP, POF and PMF, in addition to large increases in acidification and marine eutrophication (>4 times). Compared to the mono-digestion of cattle slurry (Plant adding maize silage to the process (Plant could improve the environ- mental sustainability of energy conversion from cattle slurry for those categories where the digestate application matters (i.e., GWP, AP, MEuP, PMF). However, this leads to trade-offs in other environmental categories and costs (LCC and levelised costs) because the cultivation of maize silage implies higher costs (relative to free waste feedstocks) and impacts in certain categories (e.g., land use and water depletion), as well as lower credits associated with the replacement of chemical fertilisers.

In terms of levelised costs, all biogas plants outperform heat from natural gas (75-83 % while only Plants A and B perform better than the grid electricity (26%) and Plant C generates electricity at a cost comparable to it. Furthermore, Plants A and B continue to be cost- competitive against grid power even if the heat is not exported, although this would result in about 20 % higher impacts in some categories, including GWP. To put these results in context, it is

estimated that, if all available chicken manure and 60% of cattle manure in Turkey are co-digested with other wastes, it could potentially save 12.2 Mt CO2 eq. (or 2.5 %) of annual GHG emissions and $1.3 bn (or 10%) of fossil fuel imports, as well as 37% of nitrogen, 25% of phosphate and > 100 % of potassium fertilisers consumed annually. This study indicates that biogas systems using primarily waste feed- stocks have a potential to increase the environmental and economic sustainability of energy in Turkey. Although the robustness of results has been tested considering a wide range of data and alternative scenar- ios in the sensitivity analysis, it should be noted that all these findings are dependent on the assumptions considered in the study, as well as the data used in the modelling.

To reduce the emissions from digestate application, alternative digestate treatment and application technologies should be consid- ered, such as composting, drying or evaporation of digestate. Based upon the differences in results for biogas plants in this study, feed- stock properties (e.g., biogas yield and total solids) seem to be the most crucial parameter in the sustainability of biogas plants, affect- ing each unit operation in the system. Thus, a further study should evaluate the improvement opportunities of the sustainability in bio- gas systems using cattle slurry and focus on feedstock composition (e.g., cattle slurry with different mixing ratios of alternative feed- stocks). The pre-seperation of cattle slurry can also reduce the asso- ciated impacts from the application of very dilute digestate and increase the biogas yield. Moreover, any efforts to develop sustain- able practices in maize silage cultivation (e.g., renewable power based-irrigation, organic fertilisation) could improve the sustain- ability of those plants using it.

In summary, from an environmental and economic perspective, an- aerobic digestion of agricultural wastes could help to reduce GHG emis- sions and fossil fuel import costs in Turkey, providing cleaner, more secure and affordable energy, as well as organic fertilisers. The policy recommendations to accelerate this transition are outlined below:

<list>Anaerobic digestion of wet agricultural wastes combined with the cir- cular agricultural bioeconomy should be encouraged by providing ad- ditional incentives for renewable heat and organic fertilisers. Balancing these with the existing biopower incentives would help to promote decarbonisation in a range of sectors, such as heat and agri- culture, in addition to the electricity sector. 
Organic wastes with high solid content could be utilised effectively to support the environmental and economic sustainability of biogas plants in regions where animal manure has a large potential; there- fore, the collection effort for these wastes should be increased by local authorities. 
In order to achieve maximum benefits from biogas plants, it is impor- tant to ensure the availability of the market for heat and digestate. Therefore, the region- site-specific data regarding the annual heat demand and the possibility of digestate application to nearby fields should be considered prior to the deployment of biogas plants. 
In this regard, there may be a further need for mapping heat demand across the country and developing infrastructure for district heating systems. The alternative ways of resource recovery from digestate and the effective utilisation of digestate in the crop cultivation should also be investigated and mapped across different regions of Turkey. 
Given the growing global biogas market, maize silage should also be included in Turkey's energy portfolio for its co-digestion with cattle slurry at an optimum ratio to increase the biogas yield without compromising the cost and environmental benefits. However, a fur- ther study analysing the consequential impacts of switching lands from feed to energy crop is also necessary to understand its full life cycle impacts before its wide-scale cultivation. 
It is necessary to develop a policy framework for sustainable biogas production from biomass resources and its optimal use in different end-use sectors engaging with a broad community of different stake- holders and assessing the life cycle sustainability of short-term and</list>
long-term scenarios at local, regional and national levels.

An integrated policy approach covering different sectors (i.e. energy, transport, waste and agriculture) is essential to realise all synergies and benefits associated with biogas systems. Although the scope of this analysis was limited to the biogas utilisation for heat and electric- ity generation via its combustion in a CHP plant, there might be other favourable pathways (e.g. upgrading to biomethane as an equivalent fuel to natural gas to be used in both households and vehicles) to achieve national goals pertaining to energy, economy, environment and agriculture.
